title: The 5th edition of the World Health Organization Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours: Lymphoid Neoplasms (WHO-HAEM5)
design: Consensus classification / expert review article (WHO Blue Book 5th edition overview). Not a clinical trial; defines diagnostic entities and criteria.
population: Not applicable (classification of lymphoid neoplasm entities). Relevant entity: aggressive large B-cell lymphomas, specifically DLBCL/high-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC and BCL2 rearrangements ("double-hit").
key_eligibility: Defines DLBCL/HGBL-MYC/BCL2 by the presence of DUAL rearrangements of MYC AND BCL2 (detected as gene rearrangements, e.g. by FISH break-apart), not amplification or copy-number gain
MYC/BCL6 dual-rearranged cases are explicitly EXCLUDED from the DLBCL/HGBL-MYC/BCL2 entity and reclassified as DLBCL,NOS or HGBL,NOS by morphology
Morphology may be large, intermediate, or blastoid cells; entity is defined genetically, not morphologically
HGBL-11q (formerly Burkitt-like with 11q aberration) is MYC-rearrangement-NEGATIVE and defined by the 11q gain/loss pattern; distinct from double-hit
outcomes: No efficacy outcomes (classification document). Key definitional point: the WHO-HAEM4R entity 'HGBL with dual rearrangements of MYC and BCL2 and/or BCL6' was reframed in WHO-HAEM5 to 'DLBCL/HGBL with MYC and BCL2 rearrangements,' narrowing double-hit to MYC+BCL2 rearranged only (BCL6 dual-hit removed). Notably 54% of HGBL,NOS carry a double-hit gene-expression signature (DHITsig) despite LACKING the rearrangements, underscoring that the entity requires demonstrated rearrangement.
grounds_rubric_claims: WHO-HAEM5 double-hit lymphoma = MYC + BCL2 rearrangement demonstrated by FISH (gene rearrangement), NOT MYC amplification or copy-number gain
MYC/BCL6 double-rearranged cases are no longer classified as double-hit under WHO-HAEM5
Double-hit status is defined genetically and is morphology-independent (large, intermediate, or blastoid all qualify if MYC+BCL2 rearranged)
A double-hit gene-expression signature can exist without rearrangement (HGBL,NOS) and does NOT meet the diagnostic entity definition

verbatim_quotes.quote: the WHO-HAEM5 renames the entity diffuse large B-cell lymphoma/high-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC and BCL2 rearrangements (DLBCL/HGBL-MYC/BCL2) to encompass tumours defined by the presence of dual MYC and BCL2 rearrangements that may be composed of large or intermediate or blastoid cells
verbatim_quotes.supports: Double-hit entity is defined by dual MYC AND BCL2 rearrangements (not amplification), independent of morphology

verbatim_quotes.quote: lymphoid neoplasms with dual MYC and BCL6 rearrangements represent a more diverse spectrum... these cases have been excluded from the DLBCL/HGBL-MYC/BCL2 entity and are now classified either as a subtype of DLBCL, NOS or HGBL, NOS
verbatim_quotes.supports: MYC/BCL6 dual-rearranged lymphomas are excluded from the double-hit entity in WHO-HAEM5

verbatim_quotes.quote: gene expression profiling showed that 54% of HGBL, NOS harbour the "double hit" signature (DHITsig) characteristic of LBCL/HGBL with MYC/BCL2 despite lacking rearrangements of these genes
verbatim_quotes.supports: The double-hit gene-expression signature is distinct from the rearrangement-defined entity; diagnosis requires demonstrated MYC+BCL2 rearrangement

caveats: Classification/consensus document, not comparative evidence. Does not state a specific FISH probe protocol; the point is that the entity is defined by rearrangement of MYC and BCL2, which in practice is assessed by break-apart FISH. Text does not explicitly use the word 'amplification' to contrast; the grounding is that 'rearrangement' (not gain/amplification) defines the entity.

title: Practical Assessment and Management of Vulnerabilities in Older Patients Receiving Chemotherapy: ASCO Guideline for Geriatric Oncology
design: ASCO clinical practice guideline based on a systematic review (68 studies: 30 prospective cohorts, 17 GEM RCTs, 2 GA-allocation RCTs, life-expectancy studies, and systematic reviews).
population: Patients aged 65 and older with cancer receiving chemotherapy.
line: N/A (assessment guideline; applies across treatment lines).
key_eligibility: Age >=65 receiving chemotherapy is the population for which GA is recommended (age 65 chosen because robust GA outcome data exist above that cutoff)
Candidate cohort studies required chemotherapy, >=100 patients, and outpatient GA focus
outcomes: Strong recommendation (high-quality evidence) that geriatric assessment be used in patients >=65 to identify vulnerabilities not captured by routine oncology assessment, because KPS/ECOG performance status do NOT accurately predict which older adults are at highest risk of chemotherapy adverse outcomes. Minimum domains: function, comorbidity, falls, depression, cognition, nutrition. Recommends IADLs (function), thorough history or validated tool such as CIRS-G or Charlson (comorbidity), single question (falls), GDS (depression), Mini-Cog or BOMC (cognition), weight loss/BMI (nutrition); CARG or CRASH for chemotherapy-toxicity risk; G8 or VES-13 for mortality; ePrognosis for life expectancy.
grounds_rubric_claims: Geriatric assessment should be used instead of or in addition to ECOG/KPS for patients >=65 on chemotherapy
ECOG/KPS performance status alone does not accurately identify older adults at highest risk of chemo toxicity
Comorbidity should be assessed with a validated tool such as CIRS-G or Charlson
Recommended minimum GA domains: function, comorbidity, falls, depression, cognition, nutrition

verbatim_quotes.quote: In patients age 65 and older receiving chemotherapy, geriatric assessment (GA)... should be used to identify vulnerabilities or geriatric impairments that are not routinely captured in oncology assessments (Type: Evidence-based... Evidence quality: high; Strength of recommendation: strong).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Strong recommendation for GA in patients >=65

verbatim_quotes.quote: Studies have shown that traditional oncology performance measures, such as the Karnofsky performance status (KPS) or Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status (PS) scores, do not accurately predict which older adults are at highest risk of adverse outcomes from chemotherapy.
verbatim_quotes.supports: GA over ECOG/KPS alone

verbatim_quotes.quote: Consider validated tools such as CIRS-G or Charlson. History, CIRS-G, and OARS comorbidity recommended by experts.
verbatim_quotes.supports: CIRS-G/Charlson as recommended comorbidity tools within GA

caveats: 2018 version predates the GAP70+/GAIN GA-management RCTs; recommendation for GA-guided management was informal-consensus at this point (strengthened in the 2023 update). Focused on chemotherapy, not other systemic therapies.

title: Practical Assessment and Management of Vulnerabilities in Older Patients Receiving Systemic Cancer Therapy: ASCO Guideline Update
design: ASCO guideline update; systematic review (Jan 2016-Dec 2022) identifying nine GA-guided-management (GAM) RCTs (including GAP70+ and GAIN), plus secondary analyses and systematic reviews.
population: Older adults (65+) with cancer receiving systemic therapy (chemotherapy, targeted therapy, or immunotherapy).
line: N/A (assessment/management guideline across lines and modalities).
key_eligibility: Population: older adults with cancer (65+) considering systemic (non-surgical/non-radiation) therapy
RCTs required >=100 total patients; small/pilot RCTs excluded
GAP70+ enrolled patients >=70 with incurable solid tumor or lymphoma and >=1 GA vulnerability; GAIN enrolled patients >=65 with solid tumor
outcomes: Strong recommendation (high-quality evidence): all patients 65+ with GA-identified impairments should receive GA-guided management (GAM). Driven by GAP70+ (grade 3-5 toxicity 51% intervention vs 71% usual care; RR 0.74, 95% CI 0.64-0.86, P=.0001; fewer falls) and GAIN (grade >=3 toxicity 50.5% vs 60.6%, a 10.1% reduction, P=.02). GA should include physical and cognitive function, emotional health, comorbid conditions, polypharmacy, nutrition, and social support; the Practical Geriatric Assessment (PGA) is offered as one option. Reiterates the 2018 recommendation that GA identifies vulnerabilities not captured by oncology assessment.
grounds_rubric_claims: All patients 65+ with GA-identified deficits should receive GA-guided management (strong, high-quality evidence)
GA-guided management reduces serious (grade 3-5) chemotherapy toxicity (GAP70+, GAIN RCTs)
GA should span function, cognition, emotional health, comorbidity, polypharmacy, nutrition, and social support; extends to targeted therapy and immunotherapy

verbatim_quotes.quote: All patients with cancer aged 65 and over with geriatric assessment (GA)-identified impairments should have GA-guided management (GAM) included in their care plan... (Type: Evidence based... Evidence quality: High; Strength of recommendation: Strong)
verbatim_quotes.supports: Strong recommendation for GA-guided management in 65+

verbatim_quotes.quote: a lower proportion of patients (51%) in the intervention group experienced grade 3-5 toxic effects than patients in the usual care group (71%; relative risk [RR] 0.74 (95% CI, 0.64 to 0.86; P = .0001).
verbatim_quotes.supports: GAP70+ RCT evidence that GAM reduces chemo toxicity

verbatim_quotes.quote: A GA should include high priority aging-related domains known to be associated with outcomes in older patients with cancer to include assessment of physical and cognitive function, emotional health, comorbid conditions, polypharmacy, nutrition, and social support.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Required GA domains

caveats: Evidence strongest for chemotherapy; extension to targeted/immunotherapy rests on early/indirect data. PGA is offered by informal consensus (weak recommendation) and is not yet psychometrically validated as a composite. Some GAM RCTs (e.g., 5C, EGeSOR, Orum) were negative for QOL/completion/survival endpoints.

title: Anti-CD19 CAR-T Cell Therapy in Elderly Patients: Multicentric Real-World Experience from GETH-TC/GELTAMO
design: Multicenter retrospective real-world registry study (12 Spanish centers, 2019-2023); patients >=70 vs <70, univariate + multivariate Cox/logistic regression
population: R/R aggressive LBCL treated with commercial tisa-cel or axi-cel as 3rd+ line. 412 infused: 71 (17%) were >=70 years (median 73, range 70-81), 19 (4.6%) >=75; 341 <70. Baseline disease features (stage, R-IPI, LDH, ECOG, cell of origin, primary refractory) comparable between age groups; older group had higher Charlson comorbidity index (>=3 in 77% vs 60%).
key_eligibility: R/R aggressive LBCL (DLBCL NOS, DH/TH HGBCL, transformed FL/indolent) receiving commercial CAR-T as third or subsequent line
No age ceiling — includes patients >=75; comparison by age at apheresis (18-69 vs >=70)
Primary refractory disease present in ~52-55% of both groups (NOT excluded, unlike L-MIND-type trials)
Selection criteria for elderly explicitly noted as lacking/unvalidated — motivation for the study
outcomes: Efficacy equivalent by age: month-1 ORR 69% (>=70) vs 70% (<70), P=0.97; CRR 45% vs 38%, P=0.28. 12-mo PFS 35.9% (>=70) vs 35.2% (<70), P=0.938; 12-mo OS 52.1% vs 51.1%, P=0.885. Age >=70 did NOT affect PFS (HR 0.98, P=0.941) or OS (HR 0.97, P=0.890) in multivariate analysis. Toxicity: overall CRS 84.5% vs 82% (NS), but grade >=3 CRS MORE frequent in older (15% vs 5%, P=0.002); multivariate age >=70 independent risk factor for grade 3-4 CRS (OR 3.7, 95% CI 1.3-10.5, P=0.013). Overall ICANS 42% vs 35% (P=0.281) and grade >=3 ICANS 17% vs 12% (P=0.33) — numerically higher but NOT significant. NRM low and similar (4% vs 6% at 12 mo, P=0.97).
grounds_rubric_claims: CAR-T is effective in selected older adults (>=70, incl. >=75); age >=70 does not independently worsen efficacy (PFS/OS) — no numeric age cutoff warranted on efficacy grounds
Toxicity rises with age: age >=70 is an INDEPENDENT risk factor for grade 3-4 CRS (OR 3.7) — supports age-attentive toxicity monitoring/prophylaxis, not exclusion
ICANS/neurotoxicity trends higher in older patients (42% vs 35%; grade >=3 17% vs 12%) though not significant here — consistent with age-rising neurotoxicity signal
Fitness/comorbidity composite, not age, should drive selection; disease-control factors (LDH, ECOG, bridging, primary refractory) and product (tisa vs axi) — not age — drive outcomes
Validated geriatric assessment tools are needed for candidate selection in the elderly (currently lacking)

verbatim_quotes.quote: CAR-T cell therapy in patients older than 70 years showed similar efficacy to that observed in younger patients in the real-world setting. However, age >=70 years was an independent risk factor for grades 3-4 CRS.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Efficacy preserved with age but severe CRS risk rises with age — grounds toxicity-attentive, not age-exclusionary, selection

verbatim_quotes.quote: In the multivariate analysis, age >=70 years was associated with an increased risk of grade >=3 CRS (OR 3.7, P = .013).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Age is an independent driver of severe CRS toxicity

verbatim_quotes.quote: Specifically in the elderly population, geriatric assessment tools should be validated to improve patient selection and adverse event interventions
verbatim_quotes.supports: Fitness/GA composite over chronological age; need for validated geriatric selection tools

caveats: Retrospective registry; despite higher Charlson index in older group, authors note overall comorbidity burden was relatively low, implying selection bias (only fitter elderly reached infusion). No CAR-T expansion or immune-reconstitution data. ICANS age difference did not reach significance (underpowered for neurotoxicity). Product mix (axi-cel ~55-59%) differs from Ram tisa-cel-predominant cohort, which may explain the stronger CRS-by-age signal.
line: Transplant Cell Ther 2024;30:988.e1-988.e11

title: Recent Bendamustine Treatment Before Apheresis Has a Negative Impact on Outcomes in Patients With Large B-Cell Lymphoma Receiving Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy
design: Retrospective, multicenter observational study; 7 European sites; 439 patients with R/R LBCL infused with commercial CD19 CAR T (axi-cel 58%, tisa-cel 42%), 3L+. Compared bendamustine-exposed (n=80) vs bendamustine-naive (n=359) before apheresis; washout and dose analyses. IPTW and propensity-score matching for confounder adjustment.
population: Adults with R/R LBCL receiving commercial CD19 CAR T in third or later line (Jan 2019-Oct 2022). Bendamustine-exposed patients were older (median 66 vs 61), worse ECOG, more prior lines, more transformed indolent histology (45% vs 15%).
line: Third-line or later CAR T-cell therapy (sequencing/eligibility evidence, not a treatment trial).
key_eligibility: Bendamustine exposure = >=1 cycle before apheresis; washout = time from last bendamustine dose to leukapheresis
Recent exposure defined as <9 months before apheresis (optimal discriminatory cutoff by max log-rank for OS)
All met CAR T label criteria and received flu/cy lymphodepletion
outcomes: Bendamustine-exposed vs naive: lower ORR (53% vs 72%; P<0.01), shorter PFS (3.1 vs 6.2 mo; P=0.04), shorter OS (10.3 vs 23.5 mo; P=0.01); differences attenuated after IPTW/PSM adjustment. RECENT (<9 months) exposure vs naive: ORR 40% vs 72%, PFS 1.3 vs 6.2 mo, OS 4.6 vs 23.5 mo - all P<0.01 and remained significant after IPTW (PFS HR 1.83, OS HR 2.09) and PSM. Cumulative bendamustine dose did NOT affect outcomes. Bendamustine-exposed had more grade >=3 thrombocytopenia and severe infections; CRS/ICANS rates similar. Lower CD3+ and platelet counts at apheresis; enriched for low CAR-T expanders.
grounds_rubric_claims: Recent bendamustine (<9 months) before CAR T apheresis independently worsens ORR, PFS, and OS - grounds a rule to AVOID bendamustine-containing regimens (e.g., pola-BR) before planned CAR T
Bendamustine should be avoided immediately prior to CAR T; washout of >=9 months mitigates the effect
Cumulative bendamustine dose does not matter - timing/washout is the driver, not total dose
Links to POLA-BR sequencing hazard: pola-BR (bendamustine backbone) can compromise subsequent CAR T if given recently before apheresis
caveats: Retrospective, non-randomized; bendamustine-exposed cohort had worse baseline features (confounding partially addressed by IPTW/PSM). Unadjusted differences attenuate after adjustment except for the recent (<9 mo) subset, which stays significant.

verbatim_quotes.quote: those with recent (<9 months) exposure (N = 42) displayed a lower ORR (40% v 72%; P < .01), shorter PFS (1.3 v 6.2 months; P < .01), and OS (4.6 v 23.5 months; P < .01) in comparison with bendamustine-naive patients. These differences remained significant after IPTW and PSM analysis.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Core finding: recent (<9 mo) bendamustine before apheresis independently worsens CAR T ORR/PFS/OS

verbatim_quotes.quote: Recent bendamustine exposure before apheresis was associated with negative treatment outcomes after CD19-targeted CAR T-cell therapy and should be therefore avoided in CAR T-cell candidates.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Grounds the rubric rule to avoid bendamustine before planned CAR T

verbatim_quotes.quote: the cumulative dose of bendamustine before apheresis did not affect CAR-T efficacy outcomes.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Timing/washout, not cumulative dose, drives the hazard - refines the sequencing rule

title: Second-Line Tisagenlecleucel or Standard Care in Aggressive B-Cell Lymphoma (BELINDA)
authors_year: Bishop MR, Dickinson M, Purtill D, et al. 2022
design: International phase 3, randomized (1:1) trial. Tisagenlecleucel (tisa-cel) with optional platinum-based bridging chemotherapy vs standard care (platinum salvage chemoimmunotherapy + high-dose therapy/autologous HSCT in responders). Crossover to tisa-cel permitted after confirmed SD/PD at/after week 12. Primary endpoint: event-free survival (time from randomization to SD or PD at/after week 12 assessment, or death). NEGATIVE trial.
population: Adults with aggressive B-cell lymphoma refractory to (no CR) or relapsing within 12 months after first-line anti-CD20 + anthracycline therapy; ASCT-eligible, ECOG 0-1. N=322 (tisa-cel 162 vs standard care 160). 66% refractory to first-line, 19.3% relapse <6 mo.
line: Second line (2L) R/R aggressive B-cell lymphoma
key_eligibility: Same high-risk 2L population as ZUMA-7 (refractory or relapse ≤12 mo, transplant-eligible) — yet the result was OPPOSITE (negative), so BELINDA does NOT support tisa-cel in 2L.
Optional bridging chemotherapy allowed and heavily used; median time from leukapheresis to tisa-cel infusion was 52 days (long manufacturing/logistics interval).
EFS endpoint clock started at week 12 and progressive disease before then did not count as an event, differing from ZUMA-7's definition — a design factor implicated in the null result.
Only 32.5% of standard-care patients received ASCT.
outcomes: Median EFS 3.0 months in BOTH groups; HR for event or death 1.07 (95% CI 0.82-1.40), P=0.61 — no benefit. Response at/after week 12: 46.3% (tisa-cel) vs 42.5% (standard care); CR 28.4% vs 27.5%. More PD at week 6 (preinfusion) in tisa-cel arm (25.9% vs 13.8%). CRS in 61.3% of infused patients (grade ≥3 in 5.2%); grade ≥3 neurologic events 1.9%. Conclusion: tisa-cel NOT superior to standard salvage therapy in 2L.
grounds_rubric_claims: Tisagenlecleucel (tisa-cel) is NOT indicated in the second line — BELINDA was negative (EFS HR 1.07, P=0.61)
2L CAR-T benefit is product/design-specific: unlike axi-cel (ZUMA-7) and liso-cel (TRANSFORM), tisa-cel did not beat salvage/ASCT
In an eligible 2L R/R LBCL patient, tisa-cel should not substitute for axi-cel/liso-cel or for the salvage-ASCT pathway

verbatim_quotes.quote: Tisagenlecleucel was not superior to standard salvage therapy in this trial.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Core negative conclusion: tisa-cel does not beat standard care in 2L

verbatim_quotes.quote: The median event-free survival in both groups was 3.0 months (hazard ratio for event or death in the tisagenlecleucel group, 1.07; 95% confidence interval, 0.82 to 1.40; P = 0.61).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Primary endpoint numeric result showing no EFS benefit for tisa-cel in 2L

verbatim_quotes.quote: A total of 25.9% of the patients in the tisagenlecleucel group had lymphoma progression at week 6, as compared with 13.8% of those in the standard-care group.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Longer manufacturing/time-to-infusion and preinfusion progression contributed to the null result — mechanistic contrast with axi-cel

caveats: Negative trial; do not misread as evidence FOR 2L tisa-cel. The null result is attributed partly to trial design (week-12 EFS clock, long 52-day median time-to-infusion, optional bridging masking benefit) rather than proving CAR-T is ineffective in 2L — axi-cel (ZUMA-7) and liso-cel (TRANSFORM) were positive in the same setting. Tisa-cel retains approval only for 3L+ DLBCL.

title: Epcoritamab plus GemOx in transplant-ineligible relapsed/refractory DLBCL: results from the EPCORE NHL-2 trial
authors_year: Brody JD, Jørgensen J, Belada D, et al. 2025
design: Phase 1b/2, open-label, multicenter, single-arm trial (EPCORE NHL-2, arm 5; NCT04663347). Dose-escalation + expansion; no comparator/control group. No formal hypothesis testing.
population: 103 patients with ASCT-ineligible R/R DLBCL (de novo or transformed from FL/nodal MZL, DHL/THL, FL grade 3B, or T-cell/histiocyte-rich DLBCL). Median age 72 (range 20-87), 35% aged >=75; ECOG 0-2; median 2 prior lines (38% had 1 pLOT, 62% >=2 pLOT); 52% primary refractory, 70% refractory to last therapy, 28% prior CAR T-cell therapy, 23% transformed, 61% stage IV.
line: Second-line or later (2L+) in ASCT-ineligible patients; earliest enrollment after >=1 prior therapy. 38% were treated in the 2L setting (1 prior line).
key_eligibility: Documented CD20+ DLBCL R/R to >=1 prior therapy
Previous ASCT failure OR ineligibility for ASCT due to age, performance status, comorbidities, or insufficient response to prior treatment
ECOG PS 0-2; measurable disease; adequate organ function; eligible to receive GemOx
Excluded if active infections requiring systemic therapy
outcomes: ORR 85% (88/103) and CR rate 61% (63/103) by IRC. Median PFS 11.2 mo (overall); median OS 21.6 mo overall and not reached in CR patients. Median DOR 11.7 mo; median duration of CR 23.6 mo. CR rate higher in less pretreated: 74% (1 pLOT) vs 53% (>=2 pLOT); 68% CAR-T-naive vs 45% prior CAR-T. CRS in 52% (28% G1, 23% G2, only 1% G3); CRS mostly cycle 1, all resolved, none led to discontinuation. ICANS 3% (1% G3). Grade >=3 cytopenias common (thrombocytopenia 59%, neutropenia 57%); febrile neutropenia 7%.
grounds_rubric_claims: Epcoritamab + GemOx is an evidence-supported OFF-LABEL 2L regimen for transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL: only epcoritamab MONOTHERAPY is FDA-approved (at 3L+), so the GemOx combination and use in 2L are both off-label but supported by this prospective trial

A regimen chosen off-label can still be evidence-graded as reasonable when a prospective single-arm trial demonstrates deep, durable responses (ORR 85%, CR 61%) in the exact target population (ASCT-ineligible R/R DLBCL)
Higher CR rate with fewer prior lines (74% at 1 pLOT vs 53% at >=2 pLOT) supports using the regimen earlier (2L) rather than later
Bispecific + chemo is a valid non-CAR-T option for older/less-fit patients: median age 72, one-third >=75, versus CAR-T pivotal trials (median age 56-60, no patients >76)

verbatim_quotes.quote: Epcoritamab, a CD3xCD20 bispecific antibody approved for R/R DLBCL after >=2 therapy lines, has shown promising safety and efficacy in various combinations.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Confirms the ONLY approved indication is 3L+ (after >=2 lines), so 2L use and the GemOx combination are off-label.

verbatim_quotes.quote: ORR and CR rate were 85% and 61%, respectively. Median duration of CR and OS were 23.6 and 21.6 months, respectively.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline efficacy establishing the off-label 2L regimen as an evidence-supported choice.

verbatim_quotes.quote: epcoritamab plus GemOx treatment led to higher CR rates overall (61%) and markedly improved CR rates in patients with 1 vs >=2 pLOT (74% vs 53%), supporting that this combination may help to overcome cross-resistance.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Rationale that earlier-line (2L) use improves outcomes.

verbatim_quotes.quote: CRS was primarily low grade (52% overall incidence; 28% grade 1, 23% grade 2); only 1 patient experienced grade 3 CRS.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Favorable, predominantly low-grade CRS safety profile of the off-label bispecific combination.

caveats: Single-arm, no comparator; cross-study comparisons cautioned. Enrolled during COVID-19 pandemic (13% grade 5 TEAEs, 5 were COVID-19). Combination and 2L use are off-label; regulatory approval is monotherapy at 3L+ only. Median follow-up 13.2 mo.

title: A new method of classifying prognostic comorbidity in longitudinal studies: development and validation
journal: Journal of Chronic Diseases (J Chron Dis) 40(5):373-383
design: Methods/instrument development and validation study. Weighted comorbidity index developed empirically from 1-yr mortality in an inception cohort of 559 medical inpatients (New York Hospital, 1984) and validated against 10-yr mortality in 685 breast cancer patients (Yale New Haven Hospital, 1962-1969).
population: Derivation: 559 general medical inpatients. Validation: 685 women with histologically proven primary breast carcinoma.
line: N/A (comorbidity measurement instrument, not a treatment).
key_eligibility: Derivation cohort = consecutive medical-service admissions over 1 month (99.3% of eligible)
Conditions completely resolved or history of operation for inactive conditions were NOT counted as comorbidity
Validation cohort = histologically proven primary breast cancer with first treatment at Yale New Haven Hospital
outcomes: Assigns weights (1, 2, 3, or 6) to 19 comorbid conditions summed to a total score. 1-yr mortality rose stepwise with score: '0'=12%, '1-2'=26%, '3-4'=52%, '>=5'=85%. Lymphoma and leukemia each carry a weight of 2; metastatic solid tumor and AIDS carry weight 6. Each increasing level of the index conferred a relative risk of death from comorbid disease of 2.3 (95% CI 1.9-2.8); each decade of age RR 2.4. An optional combined age-comorbidity score adds 1 point per decade over 40.
grounds_rubric_claims: Charlson Comorbidity Index is a validated instrument to quantify comorbidity burden for prognosis/eligibility decisions
A weighted comorbidity index (not a simple count of conditions) is the validated approach; conditions carry disease-specific weights
Lymphoma and leukemia are assigned a comorbidity weight of 2 in the index

verbatim_quotes.quote: A weighted index that takes into account the number and the seriousness of comorbid disease was developed in a cohort of 559 medical patients.
verbatim_quotes.supports: CCI is a weighted (not simple-count) comorbidity instrument

verbatim_quotes.quote: To simplify the system, conditions with a relative risk >=1.2 <1.5 were assigned a weight of 1; conditions with a risk >1.5 <2.5 a weight of 2; conditions with a weight of >=2.5 <3.5 a weight of 3; and those two conditions with weights of 6 or more were assigned a weight of 6.
verbatim_quotes.supports: The weighting scheme (1/2/3/6) that grounds the scoring rubric

verbatim_quotes.quote: The relative risk for each increasing level of the comorbidity index was 2.3 (95% confidence limits: 1.9-2.8) and for each decade of age was 2.4.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Validation of the index as a graded predictor of comorbid death

caveats: Derived from 1-yr mortality and tested for 10-yr prediction; authors call it 'preliminary' given small numbers per condition. Age-comorbidity composite was not separately validated. Developed in general medical and breast cancer patients, not lymphoma-specific.

title: Rating chronic medical illness burden in geropsychiatric practice and research: application of the Cumulative Illness Rating Scale (CIRS-G)
authors_year: Miller, Paradis, Houck, Mazumdar, Stack, Rifai, Mulsant, Reynolds, 1992
design: Methods/instrument feasibility and reliability study. A geriatric-adapted modification of the Cumulative Illness Rating Scale (CIRS; Linn 1968) operationalized with a manual, tested in elderly subjects.
population: 141 elderly outpatients across five groups: two geriatric medical-clinic groups (n=20 each), 45 recurrent-depressed, 21 spousally bereaved, 35 healthy controls; plus 10 inpatients for reliability testing.
line: N/A (comorbidity/illness-burden rating instrument).
key_eligibility: Elderly outpatient and inpatient geriatric/geropsychiatric subjects
Raters included nurse practitioners, physician's assistants, and geriatric psychiatrists reviewing charts/exams
outcomes: CIRS-G rates 13-14 organ-system categories each 0 (no problem) to 4 (extremely severe/urgent), yielding five summary numbers (categories endorsed, total score, severity index, and counts of level-3 and level-4 categories). Good interrater reliability: intraclass correlation 0.78 (outpatients) and 0.88 (inpatients) for total score. CIRS-G total discriminated groups (10.6 in older medical-clinic group vs 4.5 in healthy controls, p<0.0001) and correlated with ADL capability and physicians' global burden estimates. Weighted toward chronic problems, so scores are cumulative and expected to rise over time.
grounds_rubric_claims: CIRS-G is a validated organ-system-based comorbidity/illness-burden rating instrument with good interrater reliability
Each organ system is rated 0-4 by severity; the total and severity indices quantify cumulative burden
CIRS-G is one of the tools recommended for comorbidity assessment in geriatric oncology

verbatim_quotes.quote: a rating of 0 indicates no problem in a given category; a rating of 1 indicates a mild problem; a rating of 2 indicates a problem of moderate severity requiring active therapy; a rating of 3 indicates severe or constant disability; and 4, an extremely severe or urgent clinical problem.
verbatim_quotes.supports: The 0-4 per-organ-system severity scoring that grounds the rubric

verbatim_quotes.quote: Good interrater reliability (i.e., intraclass correlations of 0.78 and 0.88 in a subsample of 10 outpatients and a separate group of 10 inpatients, respectively) was achieved for CIRS(G) total scores.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Reliability of the instrument

caveats: Developed and validated in geriatric/geropsychiatric populations, not cancer patients. Small samples. A single very severe condition may be underrepresented in the total score (ceiling of one organ category).

title: Outcomes in refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma: results from the international SCHOLAR-1 study
authors_year: Crump M, Neelapu SS, Farooq U, et al. 2017
design: International, multicohort, retrospective patient-level pooled analysis. Pooled 2 phase 3 trials (CORAL, LY.12) + 2 observational cohorts (MDACC, IA/MC). Response and OS estimated from initiation of salvage therapy for refractory disease.
population: 636 patients with refractory DLBCL (incl. transformed FL and PMBCL) from 861 abstracted. Median age 55 (range 19-81); ECOG PS 0-1 in 73%; stage III-IV in 72%. Refractory categories: 28% primary refractory, 50% refractory to >=2nd-line, 22% relapsed <=12 mo post-ASCT.
line: Refractory disease at any line (pooled), analyzed from start of the next salvage line. Pre-CAR-T era benchmark.
key_eligibility: Refractory DLBCL defined as progressive disease as best response to any line, OR stable disease as best response to >=4 cycles of first-line / 2 cycles of later-line therapy, OR relapse <=12 months after ASCT
Must have received an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody and an anthracycline as a qualifying regimen
Primary CNS lymphoma excluded
outcomes: Objective response rate 26% (95% CI 21-31); CR rate 7%. Median OS 6.3 months (95% CI 5.9-7.0) from start of salvage therapy. 1-year OS 28%; 2-year OS 20%. Outcomes uniformly poor across subgroups (median OS <10 mo). Primary refractory worst (ORR 20%, CR 3%). CR after last salvage -> median OS 14.9 mo; patients who reached ASCT -> median OS 14.4 mo vs 5.1 mo without ASCT. Age <65 vs >=65 did NOT impact OS (HR 0.9, p=.37).
grounds_rubric_claims: Establishes the dismal baseline prognosis of refractory DLBCL in the pre-CAR-T era (median OS ~6 months, CR ~7%) as the historical benchmark against which any R/R DLBCL therapy must be judged
Standard salvage chemotherapy is inadequate for refractory disease (ORR 26%, CR 7%), justifying use of CAR-T / bispecifics / novel regimens over repeat chemotherapy
Chronologic age does NOT independently predict OS in refractory DLBCL (age <65 vs >=65 not significant), reinforcing that fitness, not age, should drive decisions
Primary refractory and high-IPI patients have the worst outcomes, defining highest-need subgroups

verbatim_quotes.quote: For patients with refractory DLBCL, the objective response rate was 26% (complete response rate, 7%) to the next line of therapy, and the median overall survival was 6.3 months.
verbatim_quotes.supports: The headline pre-CAR-T refractory-DLBCL benchmark (poor ORR/CR and ~6-month median OS).

verbatim_quotes.quote: the SCHOLAR-1 study provides a historical benchmark for future studies in refractory DLBCL, TFL, and PMBCL. The consistently poor outcomes shown here indicate a significant unmet need for effective therapies.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Explicitly frames the study as the benchmark of poor baseline prognosis motivating newer therapies.

verbatim_quotes.quote: Age younger than 65 years or age 65 years or older did not impact OS.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Chronologic age alone does not predict survival in refractory DLBCL, consistent with a fitness- over age-based approach.

caveats: Retrospective; pre-CAR-T era (funded by Kite Pharma). Median age (55) younger than typical real-world R/R DLBCL because pooled trial cohorts (LY.12, CORAL) had transplant-oriented eligibility. Includes TFL and PMBCL; primary CNS lymphoma excluded. Long-term survivors driven by the minority reaching ASCT and/or achieving CR/PR.

title: Therapeutic Options for Large B-Cell Lymphoma Relapsing After CD19-Directed CAR T-Cell Therapy
authors_year: El Warrak S, Kharfan-Dabaja MA, Iqbal M, Hamadani M, Chavez J, Mohty R. 2024
design: Narrative review synthesizing observational/retrospective studies and early-phase trials of salvage options after CD19 CAR-T failure in LBCL: CD19-directed agents (tafasitamab-lenalidomide, loncastuximab), polatuzumab, CD20xCD3 bispecific antibodies (epcoritamab, glofitamab, mosunetuzumab, others), allogeneic HCT, and re-treatment/next-generation CAR-T. Includes a proposed post-CAR-T treatment algorithm.
population: Adults with R/R LBCL who relapse or progress after CD19-directed CAR-T (axi-cel, tisa-cel, liso-cel). Post-CAR-T failure carries a poor prognosis (median OS ~6 months).
line: Post-CAR-T relapse (3L+ / after cellular therapy)
key_eligibility: Frames the population downstream of failed CAR-T — a distinct decision node where allo-HCT and bispecifics enter.
Allo-HCT is RESERVED for patients who relapse after CAR-T, are otherwise transplant-eligible, and achieve an objective response (CR or at least PR) to subsequent therapy.
Attainment of CR (or at least PR) prior to allografting is an explicit prerequisite for durable remission — mirrors PARMA-era chemosensitivity principle.
CD19-directed agents (tafa-len, loncastuximab) show markedly reduced efficacy after CD19 CAR-T failure (ORR ~17-34%, often CD19 antigen loss).
CD20xCD3 bispecifics (epcoritamab, glofitamab) retain meaningful activity post-CAR-T and can serve as a bridge to allo-HCT.
outcomes: Post-CAR-T failure median OS ~6 months. CD19 agents post-CAR-T: tafa-len/loncaT ORR ~17-34%, CR ~5-17%, short PFS. Bispecifics post-CAR-T: epcoritamab ORR 54.1%/CR 34.4% in prior-CAR-T subgroup; glofitamab CR 35% in prior-CAR-T. Allo-HCT after CAR-T failure (Zurko et al, N=88): 1-year OS 59%, PFS 45%, NRM 22%; disease status CR in 51% at transplant. Fried et al (N=39): 2-year OS 45%, PFS 31%, NRM 43%. Durable remission achievable only in selected, chemo/immuno-responsive, fit (median age <65) patients.
grounds_rubric_claims: Allogeneic HCT is reserved for LBCL patients who relapse after CAR-T (not used before/instead of CAR-T)
Allo-HCT after CAR-T requires achieving a CR (or at least PR) to subsequent therapy as a prerequisite for durable benefit
CD19-directed agents lose efficacy after CD19 CAR-T failure (antigen loss/downregulation); non-CD19 bispecifics are preferred and can bridge to allo-HCT
Post-CAR-T relapse is a poor-prognosis state (median OS ~6 months) warranting clinical-trial enrollment when available

verbatim_quotes.quote: Prior to the approval and widespread use of CAR T in R/R LBCL, allo-HCT was generally reserved for patients who relapsed after an auto-HCT and/or had failed multiple prior lines of therapy.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Allo-HCT is positioned as a later-line, post-cellular-therapy option — reserved, not front-loaded

verbatim_quotes.quote: One of the main limitations for allo-HCT is the prerequisite of achieving disease control in the form of CR or at least PR.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Chemosensitivity/response prerequisite for allo-HCT after CAR-T failure

verbatim_quotes.quote: relapsing after CAR Ts or failure to respond is generally associated with a poor prognosis with an anticipated median overall survival (OS) of approximately 6 months, at best
verbatim_quotes.supports: Post-CAR-T failure is a poor-prognosis decision node (median OS ~6 months)

caveats: Review-level (not a controlled trial); allo-HCT and bispecific data are largely retrospective/single-arm with selection bias toward younger, fitter, response-achieving patients (median age <65). No head-to-head data define optimal sequencing; authors recommend clinical-trial enrollment first, then bispecific/ADC/mAb (ideally targeting a non-CD19 antigen) with allo-HCT consolidation in eligible responders.

title: Epcoritamab, a Novel Subcutaneous CD3xCD20 Bispecific T-Cell-Engaging Antibody, in Relapsed or Refractory Large B-Cell Lymphoma: Dose Expansion (EPCORE NHL-1)
design: Phase 1/2, single-arm, multicenter, open-label dose-expansion cohort (EPCORE NHL-1; NCT03625037). Subcutaneous epcoritamab treat-to-progression with step-up dosing; 48 mg full dose. No B-cell-depleting pretreatment; corticosteroid CRS prophylaxis. Sponsors Genmab/AbbVie. Basis for 3L+ accelerated approval.
population: 157 patients with R/R CD20+ LBCL and >=2 prior lines. Median age 64 (range 20-83); median 3 prior lines (range 2-11); 61.1% primary refractory; 38.9% prior CAR-T (75.4% of those refractory to CAR-T); 19.7% prior ASCT; 88.5% DLBCL.
line: 3L+ (>=2 prior lines including anti-CD20; prior ASCT failure or ineligibility required)
key_eligibility: Age >=18, CD20+ DLBCL or other aggressive NHL (PMBCL, HGBCL, FL grade 3B)
>=2 prior lines of systemic therapy including >=1 anti-CD20-containing regimen
Prior failure of OR ineligibility for autologous stem-cell transplantation
Prior CAR-T permitted and included (38.9%); primary-refractory NOT excluded (61.1% of cohort)
ECOG 0-2; no minimum life expectancy or leukocyte count required
outcomes: Primary endpoint ORR by IRC = 63.1% (95% CI 55.0-70.6); CR rate 38.9% (95% CI 31.2-46.9). Median DoR 12.0 mo (among complete responders: not reached; 88.7% of CRs in response at 6 and 9 mo). Median time to response 1.4 mo; time to CR 2.7 mo. Median PFS 4.4 mo (95% CI 3.0-7.9); PFS not reached in complete responders. Median OS not reached. MRD-negative 45.8%. Prior-CAR-T subgroup (n=61): ORR 54.1%, CR 34.4%. Primary-refractory (n=96): ORR 55.2%, CR 30.2%. CRS 49.7% (grade 1-2 47.1%, grade 3 2.5%, no grade 4/5); ICANS 6.4% with one fatal event; grade >=3 AEs 61.1%; discontinuation for AE 7.6%.
grounds_rubric_claims: Epcoritamab monotherapy is an approved 3L+ option for R/R LBCL (>=2 prior lines) -- EPCORE NHL-1 is the approval basis
Subcutaneous, treat-to-progression bispecific (contrast with fixed-duration glofitamab)
Requires prior ASCT failure or ineligibility and prior anti-CD20 exposure
Retains activity after prior CAR-T (ORR 54%, CR 34%) and in primary-refractory disease (ORR 55%, CR 30%; both included, not excluded)
CRS is mostly low grade (grade 3 2.5%) with corticosteroid prophylaxis and 24-h monitoring at first full dose; one fatal ICANS event
Depth of response matters: CR-vs-nonresponder PFS HR 0.11

verbatim_quotes.quote: adults with relapsed or refractory CD20+ large B-cell lymphoma and at least two prior therapy lines (including anti-CD20 therapies) received subcutaneous epcoritamab in 28-day cycles... until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.
verbatim_quotes.supports: 3L+ line positioning and treat-to-progression (non-fixed-duration) design

verbatim_quotes.quote: the overall response rate was 63.1% (95% CI, 55.0 to 70.6) and the complete response rate was 38.9% (95% CI, 31.2 to 46.9).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline ORR and CR (approval-basis efficacy)

verbatim_quotes.quote: In patients with primary refractory disease (n = 96), the ORR was 55.2% and the CR rate was 30.2%. In patients who received prior CAR T-cell therapy (n = 61), the ORR was 54.1% and the CR rate was 34.4%.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Activity in primary-refractory and post-CAR-T subgroups (both included, unlike L-MIND)

caveats: Single-arm, no control group; median follow-up only 10.7 mo. One fatal ICANS event (with confounders). First full dose requires 24-h inpatient monitoring. Regional ORR differences driven by CAR-T-exposure proportions.

title: MATRix-RICE therapy and autologous haematopoietic stem-cell transplantation in diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with secondary CNS involvement (MARIETTA)
design: International, single-arm, phase 2 trial; 24 hospitals in Italy, UK, Netherlands, Switzerland (IELSG). Modified intention-to-treat analysis. NCT02329080.
population: Adults 18-70 years with histologically proven DLBCL and secondary CNS involvement (brain, meninges, cranial nerves, eyes, spinal cord) either at initial diagnosis (concomitant with systemic disease) or at relapse; ECOG PS <=3. 79 enrolled, 75 assessable; median age 58. 32 (43%) CNS at presentation, 43 registered at relapse; 20/43 (47%) systemic disease refractory to prior line. Regimen: 3x MATRix (rituximab, high-dose methotrexate 3.5 g/m2, cytarabine, thiotepa) then 3x RICE, then carmustine-thiotepa conditioned autologous HSCT; intrathecal chemo in all.
key_eligibility: Double-hit and triple-hit lymphoma patients were ELIGIBLE (explicitly included), as were high-grade transformations from indolent lymphoma
No previous treatment with high-dose methotrexate allowed (HD-MTX-naive required)
Primary CNS lymphoma EXCLUDED (this is a secondary-CNS trial); also excluded prior organ transplant/immunosuppression, HIV
Any prior systemic-lymphoma therapy allowed except HD-MTX or prior autologous/allogeneic HSCT
Both untreated (CNS at diagnosis) and relapsed patients included
outcomes: Primary endpoint MET: 1-year PFS 58% (95% CI 55-61) for whole assessable population (42/75 progression-free at 1 yr). ORR after MATRix-RICE 65% (95% CI 54-76), CR 39%. End-of-programme ORR 61% (CR 55%), median DOR 26 months. 37 patients received autologous HSCT. 2-year OS 46% (95% CI 39-53) overall, 83% for transplanted. Subgroup: 2-year PFS 71% for CNS-at-initial-diagnosis vs 28% for CNS-at-relapse (p=0.0031). Grade 3-4 toxicity mainly haematologic (neutropenia 61%, thrombocytopenia 60%, anaemia 35%); treatment-related mortality 5% (4 Gram-negative sepsis deaths).
grounds_rubric_claims: MARIETTA (MATRix-RICE + autologous HSCT) is the largest prospective trial and reference regimen for secondary CNS lymphoma in DLBCL
Secondary CNS lymphoma treatment uses CNS-penetrant drugs: high-dose methotrexate, high-dose cytarabine, and thiotepa (MATRix), plus thiotepa-based conditioning
Double-hit/triple-hit DLBCL patients are eligible for MARIETTA-type intensive CNS-directed therapy
Outcomes differ by timing: CNS involvement at initial diagnosis has substantially better PFS than CNS relapse
Intrathecal chemotherapy is a standard component and was associated with improved PFS

verbatim_quotes.quote: Patients with high-grade transformation from indolent lymphoma and patients with double-hit or triple-hit lymphoma were eligible.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Double-hit/triple-hit DLBCL with secondary CNS involvement were explicitly eligible for MATRix-RICE + autologous HSCT

verbatim_quotes.quote: At 1 year from enrolment the primary endpoint was met, 42 patients were progression free (progression-free survival 58%; 95% CI 55-61).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline efficacy: 1-year PFS 58% in secondary CNS DLBCL

verbatim_quotes.quote: the main inclusion criteria were: histologically proven diagnosis of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma; CNS involvement... at presentation... or at relapse... and no previous treatment with high-dose methotrexate
verbatim_quotes.supports: Key eligibility: DLBCL with secondary CNS involvement, HD-MTX-naive; PCNSL excluded

verbatim_quotes.quote: patients with CNS localisation at initial diagnosis had a significantly improved progression-free survival compared with patients with CNS involvement at relapse, with a 2-year progression free survival of 71%... and 28%... (p=0.0031)
verbatim_quotes.supports: Timing of CNS involvement (diagnosis vs relapse) is prognostic for MARIETTA outcomes

caveats: Single-arm phase 2 (no randomized comparator). Very poor-prognosis population; salvage after MARIETTA failure was futile (median survival ~1 month post-progression). Excludes PCNSL and HIV/immunosuppressed. Only 37/75 reached transplant, so intent-to-treat outcomes are lower than transplanted-subgroup figures.

title: Glofitamab for Relapsed or Refractory Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma
design: Phase 2 part of an open-label phase 1-2 trial (NCT03075696); single-arm, fixed-duration glofitamab monotherapy (12 cycles) with obinutuzumab pretreatment. Sponsor F. Hoffmann-La Roche.
population: 155 patients with R/R DLBCL (NOS), transformed FL, high-grade B-cell lymphoma, or PMBCL who had received >=2 prior lines. Median age 66 (range 21-90); median 3 prior lines (range 2-7); 33% prior CAR-T; 58% primary refractory; 86% refractory to last therapy.
line: 3L+ (third line and beyond; >=2 prior lines including anti-CD20 and anthracycline)
key_eligibility: Histologically confirmed DLBCL NOS, transformed FL, HGBCL, or PMBCL
Relapsed after or refractory to >=2 prior lines including >=1 anti-CD20 antibody-containing regimen and >=1 anthracycline-containing regimen
ECOG 0-1
Prior CAR-T permitted and included (33% of cohort); primary-refractory NOT excluded (58% primary refractory)
outcomes: Primary endpoint CR by IRC = 39% (95% CI 32-48). ORR 52% (95% CI 43-60). CR among prior-CAR-T patients 35%. Median time to CR 42 days. CR ongoing at 12 mo in 78%. Median DoCR not reached (95% CI 16.8-NR). Median PFS 4.9 mo; 12-mo PFS 37%. 12-mo OS 50%. Median OS 11.5 mo (investigator). CRS in 63% (grade >=3 in 4%); ICANS-consistent neuro events 8% (grade >=3 in 3%). Grade >=3 AEs 62%; discontinuation for AE 9%.
grounds_rubric_claims: Glofitamab is an approved fixed-duration (12-cycle) monotherapy option for R/R DLBCL at 3L+ (>=2 prior lines)
Glofitamab retains activity after prior CAR-T (CR 35% in prior-CAR-T subgroup)
Fixed-duration bispecific: treatment stops at 12 cycles rather than treat-to-progression, unlike epcoritamab/odronextamab
Requires obinutuzumab pretreatment + step-up dosing for CRS mitigation; CRS mostly low-grade and confined to cycle 1
Eligibility requires prior anti-CD20 and anthracycline exposure

verbatim_quotes.quote: In the phase 2 part of a phase 1-2 study, we enrolled patients with relapsed or refractory DLBCL who had received at least two lines of therapy previously.
verbatim_quotes.supports: 3L+ line-of-therapy positioning (>=2 prior lines)

verbatim_quotes.quote: Patients received pretreatment with obinutuzumab to mitigate cytokine release syndrome, followed by fixed-duration glofitamab monotherapy (12 cycles total).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Fixed-duration monotherapy design with obinutuzumab pretreatment

verbatim_quotes.quote: 39% (95% confidence interval [CI], 32 to 48) of the patients had a complete response according to independent review. Results were consistent among the 52 patients who had previously received chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy (35% of whom had a complete response).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline CR rate and retained efficacy after prior CAR-T

caveats: Single-arm, no comparator; historical control benchmark of 20% CR. Conducted primarily in Europe/Australia with limited racial diversity. Five fatal AEs were Covid-19-related. Cross-trial comparisons cautioned by authors.

title: CAR T-cell Therapy for Central Nervous System Lymphoma
design: Narrative review of published CAR T-cell studies in primary and secondary CNS lymphoma (compiles ~13 primary studies + meta-analyses; predominantly retrospective, non-randomized).
population: Highly pretreated primary (PCNSL) and secondary (SCNSL) CNS lymphoma, mostly DLBCL; younger than typical CNSL median age, median 3+ prior lines, variable prior ASCT. Products: axi-cel, tisa-cel, liso-cel, relma-cel, brexu-cel. Pivotal CAR-T DLBCL trials had EXCLUDED CNS involvement due to neurotoxicity fears.
key_eligibility: CNS lymphoma patients were historically EXCLUDED from pivotal CAR-T trials (ZUMA, TRANSFORM, etc.) because of fear of neurotoxicity - this review addresses that evidence gap with off-trial/retrospective data
Includes both PCNSL and SCNSL; largest series focus on SCNSL because CAR-T is already licensed in SCNSL in routine practice in many countries
Some studies included patients with prior CNS involvement but no active CNS disease at infusion
outcomes: Across studies ORR 64-95%, CR 32-67%. 1-year PFS ranged 15-25% (some studies) to >40% (others); e.g. Choquet PCNSL 12-month PFS 43%, Nayak axi-cel median PFS 14.3 mo / OS 26.4 mo. ICANS in 36-68% (grade 3-4 in 4.5-29%); CRS in 58-89% (grade 3-4 in 0-21%) - comparable to systemic DLBCL. Disease control before infusion (CR/PR vs SD/PD) strongly predicted outcome (e.g. Choquet 1-yr relapse-free 79% if CR/PR vs 24% if SD/PD, p=0.02).
grounds_rubric_claims: CAR T-cell therapy is active in secondary CNS DLBCL with response rates approaching systemic DLBCL, supported by non-randomized/retrospective evidence
Neurotoxicity (ICANS) with CAR-T in CNS lymphoma is not higher than in systemic DLBCL and should not preclude use
CNS involvement was excluded from pivotal CAR-T trials, so CNS CAR-T evidence is non-randomized (proof-of-concept series, cohorts, meta-analyses)
Disease control/bridging to achieve response before CAR-T infusion improves outcomes in CNS lymphoma

verbatim_quotes.quote: All studies reported high response rates, with overall response rates ranging from 64 to 95% and CR rates ranging from 32 to 67%.
verbatim_quotes.supports: CAR-T efficacy in CNS lymphoma: ORR 64-95%, CR 32-67%

verbatim_quotes.quote: CNS lymphomas have been excluded from such trials partly because of their poor prognosis, but mostly because of the fear of neurotoxicity.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Pivotal CAR-T trials excluded CNS lymphoma; evidence base is non-randomized

verbatim_quotes.quote: the results appeared reassuring in all the studies, with 36 to 68% of ICANS and 4.5 to 29% of grade 3-4 ICANS, which is also close to what is reported in systemic DLBCL
verbatim_quotes.supports: Neurotoxicity rates in CNS-lymphoma CAR-T are comparable to systemic DLBCL

caveats: Narrative review; underlying studies mostly retrospective, heterogeneous in populations/products/prior therapy, with limited follow-up. No randomized data for CAR-T in CNS lymphoma. Prognosis after post-CAR-T relapse is poor (median OS ~4.6 mo). Position of CAR-T in the treatment sequence remains undefined.

title: The International Consensus Classification of Mature Lymphoid Neoplasms: a report from the Clinical Advisory Committee
design: Consensus classification / diagnostic-criteria report from an international Clinical Advisory Committee (14 working groups), updating lymphoid neoplasm classification with genomic data.
population: N/A (diagnostic classification of mature lymphoid, histiocytic, and dendritic cell neoplasms).
line: N/A (pathology/diagnostic-criteria document).
key_eligibility: Defines diagnostic entities and required ancillary studies (e.g., FISH) for classification
High-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC and BCL2 rearrangements category redefined to EXCLUDE cases with only MYC and BCL6 rearrangements (those become a separate provisional entity)
outcomes: Establishes 'High-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC and BCL2 rearrangements' (HGBCL-DH-BCL2, 'double-hit') as a defined entity of GCB origin, diagnosed by demonstrating both MYC and BCL2 rearrangements (translocations detected by FISH). Critically, copy-number increase or amplification of MYC/BCL2 is explicitly NOT sufficient to substitute for a true rearrangement. Double-hit categories continue to exclude follicular lymphoma; the MYC partner gene (IG vs non-IG) does not affect classification. HGBCL-DH-BCL6 (MYC+BCL6) is a separate provisional entity.
grounds_rubric_claims: Double-hit lymphoma = concurrent MYC AND BCL2 rearrangements (translocations), demonstrated by FISH
Copy-number gain or amplification of MYC/BCL2 does NOT qualify as a rearrangement and cannot substitute for it in the double-hit definition
MYC+BCL6 (without BCL2) is a separate provisional entity, not the classic MYC/BCL2 double-hit

verbatim_quotes.quote: HGBCL-DH now comprises 2 groups: HGBCL with MYC and BCL2 rearrangements (with or without BCL6 rearrangement) (HGBCL-DH-BCL2) and a new provisional entity, HBGBL with MYC and BCL6 rearrangements (HGBCL-DH-BCL6).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Double-hit defined by MYC and BCL2 rearrangement (distinct from MYC/BCL6)

verbatim_quotes.quote: Neither copy number increase nor amplification of these genes is sufficient to substitute for rearrangement in these categories.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Amplification/copy-number gain does NOT count as double-hit; only true rearrangement qualifies

verbatim_quotes.quote: The category is redefined to exclude patients with only MYC and BCL6 rearrangements.
verbatim_quotes.supports: MYC/BCL6-only cases are excluded from the classic MYC+BCL2 double-hit category

caveats: ICC classification runs parallel to (and diverges in places from) the concurrent WHO-HAEM5; terminology and provisional-entity status (e.g., HGBCL-DH-BCL6) may differ between systems. Requires FISH; MYC partner-gene significance remains controversial.

title: Geriatric Assessment-Identified Deficits in Older Cancer Patients With Normal Performance Status
design: Cross-sectional analysis of a prospective registry (Carolina Senior, NCT01137825); brief geriatric assessment vs. Karnofsky performance status
population: 984 cancer patients aged >=65 (mean 73, range 65-99); analysis subsample of 796 (81%) rated functionally normal (KPS >=80) by BOTH professional and self-report. 74% female, 89% white; 6% lymphoma, 5% leukemia (mostly breast, 54%). Not R/R DLBCL-specific, but the frailty-vs-PS principle is directly transferable.
key_eligibility: English-speaking cancer patients aged >=65 enrolled in Carolina Senior registry
Completed brief geriatric assessment (KPS + Patient-KPS + multi-domain measures)
Focus subsample required agreement between professional and patient KPS >=80 (excludes disagreement to isolate GA signal beyond PS)
outcomes: Among KPS >=80 (functionally normal) patients: 69% had >=1 GA-identified deficit; only ~1/3 had none. 24% had >=3 deficits. Specific deficits: 43% took >=9 medications daily; 25% had >=4 comorbidities; 23% >=1 IADL impairment; 18% Timed Up-and-Go >=14 s (fall/early-death predictor) or unable; 18% >=5% unintentional weight loss; 12% MHI-5 <=76 (poor mental health). Multivariable: each 5-yr age increase OR 1.2 (95% CI 1.06-1.38) for having a deficit; active treatment OR 1.7 (1.13-2.64).
grounds_rubric_claims: ECOG/KPS performance status systematically under-captures frailty; a functionally normal PS score does not exclude clinically meaningful deficits
Multi-domain geriatric assessment surfaces modifiable/prognostic impairments (polypharmacy, comorbidity, IADL, gait/TUG, weight loss, cognition, mood) that PS misses
TUG >=14 s and >=5% unintentional weight loss are single deficits with independent prognostic weight even in a PS-normal older patient
Older age independently raises the odds of hidden deficits, supporting GA over chronological age or PS alone for treatment decisions

verbatim_quotes.quote: Within this sample of older cancer patients who were rated as functionally normal by KPS, GA identified important deficits that could affect treatment tolerance and outcomes.
verbatim_quotes.supports: PS-normal does not equal deficit-free; GA adds information beyond performance status

verbatim_quotes.quote: Only a third of our functionally normal sample had no GA-identified deficits.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Magnitude of PS under-capture: 69% of KPS >=80 patients had at least one deficit

verbatim_quotes.quote: The odds ratio for each 5-year increase in age was 1.2 (95% CI: 1.06-1.38), suggesting that as age increases, so do the odds of having at least 1 deficit.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Age independently predicts hidden frailty deficits beyond performance status

caveats: Predominantly breast cancer (54%) and female (74%); only 6% lymphoma. Not treatment-outcome data and not R/R DLBCL or CAR-T specific; grounds the PS-vs-frailty principle, not any lymphoma-specific numeric threshold. Uses KPS (not ECOG) as the PS instrument.
line: The Oncologist 2015;20:379-385

title: A focus on pharmacotherapy for primary central nervous system lymphoma
design: Editorial/narrative review of PCNSL pharmacotherapy, summarizing the (limited) randomized and non-comparative evidence.
population: Primary CNS lymphoma (PCNSL), the vast majority of which are DLBCL. Discusses CNS penetration of chemotherapeutic agents and HD-MTX-based regimens.
key_eligibility: Not a trial; reviews which drugs cross the intact blood-brain/blood-CSF barrier - a defining constraint for CNS-directed therapy
CNS penetration (CSF concentration as surrogate): high-dose cytarabine, temozolomide, and thiotepa reach >100% of plasma; ifosfamide ~30%; high-dose methotrexate <5% but still cytotoxic
Standard aggressive-lymphoma CHOP-type regimens are inadequate for CNS disease because of poor CNS penetration
outcomes: No new data (review). Key grounding facts: HD-MTX is the single most important drug in PCNSL (optimally >3 g/m2 at <=2-week intervals, 3-4 h infusion). HD-MTX monotherapy less effective than HD-MTX-based polychemotherapy. Randomized phase 2 (Ferreri 2009): HD-MTX + high-dose cytarabine vs HD-MTX alone gave CR 46% vs 18% (p=0.006) and 3-year PFS 38% vs 21% (p=0.01). Rituximab penetrates CNS poorly (<1% of systemic concentration in leptomeningeal compartment). Thiotepa-based high-dose chemotherapy + ASCT consolidation feasible in younger patients (3-yr PFS ~63-79%). G-PCNSL-SG1: consolidating WBRT gave no survival advantage and increased neurotoxicity.
grounds_rubric_claims: CNS-penetrant drugs are required for CNS lymphoma: high-dose methotrexate, high-dose cytarabine, and thiotepa are the CNS-penetrant backbone (thiotepa and high-dose cytarabine reach >100% of plasma CSF levels; HD-MTX <5% but cytotoxic)
Standard CHOP-type regimens are inadequate for CNS lymphoma due to poor blood-brain-barrier penetration
HD-MTX is the most important drug in PCNSL; combining HD-MTX with high-dose cytarabine improves CR and PFS (randomized phase 2)
Rituximab penetrates the CNS poorly (<1% of systemic concentration reaches the leptomeninges)
Thiotepa-based conditioning + ASCT is a valid consolidation - supporting the CNS-penetrant drug choices in MARIETTA-type regimens

verbatim_quotes.quote: The CSF concentrations (regarded a surrogate marker for CNS penetration) are >100% of the corresponding plasma concentrations for high-dose cytarabine (HDAraC), temozolomide and thiotepa, approximately 30% for ifosfamide and <5%, but still cytotoxic, for high-dose methotrexate (HDMTX).
verbatim_quotes.supports: CNS-penetrant drug ranking: high-dose cytarabine, temozolomide, thiotepa >100% plasma; HD-MTX <5% but cytotoxic

verbatim_quotes.quote: the cyclophosphamide, vincristine, doxorubicine, prednisolone regimen, the current standard chemotherapy for aggressive lymphomas, is not an adequate treatment of PCNSL
verbatim_quotes.supports: CHOP-type standard lymphoma chemotherapy is inadequate for CNS lymphoma (poor CNS penetration)

verbatim_quotes.quote: as a large protein, rituximab poorly penetrates the CNS, with less than 1% of systemic concentration reaching the leptomeningeal compartment
verbatim_quotes.supports: Rituximab has poor CNS penetration (<1% of systemic concentration in the leptomeninges)

caveats: 2015 editorial - predates CAR-T era and current regimens; reflects PCNSL (not secondary CNS) but the CNS-penetration pharmacology (HD-MTX/ara-C/thiotepa) generalizes. Opinion piece; author flags the Ferreri 2009 randomized phase 2 monotherapy arm may have used a suboptimal HD-MTX schedule.

title: Tafasitamab plus lenalidomide in relapsed or refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (L-MIND): a multicentre, prospective, single-arm, phase 2 study
design: Multicentre, open-label, single-arm phase 2 study; 35 sites in 10 countries; NCT02399085. Tafasitamab (anti-CD19 mAb) 12 mg/kg IV + lenalidomide 25 mg/day for up to 12 cycles, then tafasitamab monotherapy until progression. Safety n=81; primary activity analysis n=80. Primary endpoint: centrally assessed ORR (2007 IWG criteria).
population: Adults with R/R DLBCL after 1-3 prior systemic regimens (>=1 anti-CD20), ASCT-INELIGIBLE. Median age 72; 50% had only 1 prior line; all had prior anti-CD20 and anthracycline. Median 2 prior lines.
line: ASCT-ineligible R/R DLBCL, 2L-4L (1-3 prior regimens).
key_eligibility: PRIMARY-REFRACTORY DLBCL EXCLUDED (defined as no response to, or progression during/within 6 months of, frontline therapy) - the key exclusion grounding a rubric rule that L-MIND evidence does NOT apply to primary-refractory patients
Double-hit/triple-hit DLBCL excluded (if known)
Only 1-3 prior lines allowed; must be ASCT-ineligible; prior anti-CD19 or immunomodulatory drugs (thalidomide/lenalidomide) excluded
CNS lymphoma involvement excluded
Pre-amendment: only relapse within 3 months of prior anti-CD20 counted as primary refractory; post-amendment aligned to 6-month NCCN definition
outcomes: ORR 60% (95% CI 48-71); CR 43% (32-54); PR 18%. Median DoR 21.7 months (not reached for CR; 93% of CR patients had response lasting 18 months). Median PFS 12.1 months; 12-month PFS 50%; median OS not reached (12-month OS 74%). Grade >=3 TEAEs: neutropenia 48%, thrombocytopenia 17%, febrile neutropenia 12%. Toxicity decreased after lenalidomide discontinuation (tafasitamab monotherapy phase).
grounds_rubric_claims: Tafasitamab-lenalidomide (L-MIND) is an option for ASCT-ineligible R/R DLBCL, ORR 60%/CR 43%, durable responses
L-MIND EXCLUDED primary-refractory DLBCL - so its efficacy evidence does NOT support use in primary-refractory patients (key rubric guardrail)
L-MIND excluded double/triple-hit and limited to 1-3 prior lines - eligibility caveats on generalizability
Anti-CD19 mAb - relevant to CD19-directed sequencing considerations
caveats: Single-arm; enriched for less-refractory patients (primary-refractory excluded), which inflates apparent benefit relative to real-world 3L+ populations. Small n=80.

verbatim_quotes.quote: Exclusion criteria included ... primary refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, defined as no response to, or progression during or within 6 months of frontline therapy
verbatim_quotes.supports: L-MIND explicitly EXCLUDED primary-refractory DLBCL - the load-bearing exclusion for a rubric rule limiting tafasitamab-lenalidomide applicability

verbatim_quotes.quote: 48 (60%; 95% CI 48-71) of 80 patients who received tafasitamab plus lenalidomide had an objective response: 34 (43%; 32-54) had a complete response and 14 (18%; 10-28) had a partial response.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline efficacy of tafasitamab-lenalidomide in ASCT-ineligible R/R DLBCL

verbatim_quotes.quote: were not candidates for high-dose chemotherapy and subsequent autologous stem-cell transplantation
verbatim_quotes.supports: Population is ASCT-ineligible R/R DLBCL

title: Axicabtagene Ciloleucel as Second-Line Therapy for Large B-Cell Lymphoma (ZUMA-7)
authors_year: Locke FL, Miklos DB, Jacobson CA, et al. 2022
design: Phase 3, randomized (1:1), open-label, international superiority trial. Axi-cel (autologous anti-CD19 CAR-T) vs standard of care (platinum-based salvage chemoimmunotherapy followed by high-dose therapy + ASCT in responders). Randomization stratified by response to first-line therapy (primary refractory vs relapse ≤6 mo vs relapse >6-≤12 mo) and second-line age-adjusted IPI. Primary endpoint: event-free survival by blinded central review. Note: the local full-text file is the trial protocol + statistical analysis plan supplement, which grounds design and eligibility verbatim; headline efficacy numbers below are the published ZUMA-7 primary results.
population: Adults with R/R DLBCL after first-line rituximab + anthracycline-based chemoimmunotherapy; disease either primary-refractory or relapsed within 12 months of initiating first-line therapy. Transplant-intended population (intent to proceed to HDT/ASCT if second-line response). N=359 randomized (axi-cel 180 vs SOC 179).
line: Second line (2L) R/R LBCL
key_eligibility: INCLUDES the highest-risk 2L population: primary-refractory disease AND early relapse (≤12 months) — the opposite of L-MIND, which excludes primary-refractory. This is the population where 2L CAR-T is indicated.
Refractory defined as no complete remission to first-line therapy (PD as best response, or SD after ≥4 cycles); patients merely intolerant to first-line therapy were excluded.
Relapsed disease defined as CR to first-line followed by biopsy-proven relapse ≤12 months of initiating first-line therapy.
Required adequate first-line therapy: anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody (unless CD20-negative) AND an anthracycline-containing regimen.
Intent to proceed to HDT and ASCT if response to second-line therapy (i.e., otherwise transplant-eligible/intended patients).
Exclusion: history of autologous or allogeneic stem cell transplant.
outcomes: Published ZUMA-7 primary results: median EFS 8.3 vs 2.0 months, HR 0.40 (95% CI 0.31-0.51), P<0.001; 24-month EFS 41% vs 16%. ORR 83% vs 50%; CR 65% vs 32%. Only ~35% of SOC arm proceeded to ASCT. Axi-cel grade ≥3 CRS 6%, grade ≥3 neurologic events 21%. Establishes 2L axi-cel superior to salvage chemo/ASCT for primary-refractory/early-relapse LBCL.
grounds_rubric_claims: 2L CAR-T (axi-cel) is superior to salvage chemotherapy + ASCT for primary-refractory or early-relapse (<12 mo) LBCL on EFS
Primary-refractory / early-relapse (<12 mo) LBCL is an indication for 2L CAR-T rather than salvage/ASCT
2L axi-cel eligibility requires prior anti-CD20 + anthracycline first-line therapy and transplant intent

verbatim_quotes.quote: A Phase 3, Randomized, Open-Label Study Evaluating the Efficacy of Axicabtagene Ciloleucel versus Standard of Care Therapy in Subjects with Relapsed/Refractory Diffuse Large B Cell Lymphoma (ZUMA-7)
verbatim_quotes.supports: Design: head-to-head randomized comparison of 2L axi-cel vs standard salvage-of-care in R/R DLBCL

verbatim_quotes.quote: Randomization will be stratified by response to first-line therapy (primary refractory, vs relapse ≤ 6 months of initiating first-line therapy vs relapse > 6 and ≤ 12 months of initiating first-line therapy)
verbatim_quotes.supports: Population = primary-refractory or relapse within 12 months (the early-relapse/refractory group where 2L CAR-T applies)

verbatim_quotes.quote: Refractory disease defined as no complete remission to first-line therapy; subjects who are intolerant to first-line therapy are excluded
verbatim_quotes.supports: Key eligibility definition of primary-refractory disease grounding 2L CAR-T indication

verbatim_quotes.quote: Intent to proceed to HDT and ASCT if response to second-line therapy
verbatim_quotes.supports: Transplant-intended population — axi-cel compared directly against the salvage-chemo-then-ASCT pathway

caveats: Local file is the ZUMA-7 protocol + SAP supplement (not the results paper); it grounds design, stratification, and eligibility verbatim. The efficacy numbers (EFS HR 0.40, median 8.3 vs 2.0 mo, ORR 83%/CR 65%) are the published primary results and should be cited to the NEJM article body, not to the protocol text.

title: Loncastuximab tesirine in relapsed or refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (LOTIS-2): a multicentre, open-label, single-arm, phase 2 trial
design: Multicentre (28 sites, USA/UK/Italy/Switzerland), open-label, single-arm phase 2 trial; NCT03589469. Loncastuximab tesirine (CD19-directed antibody-drug conjugate, PBD payload) 150 ug/kg x2 cycles then 75 ug/kg, up to 1 year. As-treated population n=145. Primary endpoint: centrally assessed ORR.
population: Adults with R/R DLBCL after >=2 prior multiagent systemic therapies; ECOG 0-2; measurable disease. Included high-risk groups: double/triple-hit, transformed, and primary-refractory DLBCL. Median 3 prior lines; 9% prior CAR T; median age 66.
line: Third-line or later (3L+); >=2 prior systemic therapies.
key_eligibility: Broad high-risk eligibility: double-hit/triple-hit, transformed, and primary-refractory DLBCL all included (unlike L-MIND/pola-BR)
Biopsy-proven CD19 expression required for patients with previous CD19-directed therapy - relevant to antigen-loss sequencing
Active CNS lymphoma excluded; bulky disease (>=10 cm) excluded after protocol amendment 2
Previous CAR T-cell therapy allowed (9% had prior CAR T)
outcomes: As-treated (n=145): ORR 48.3% (95% CI 39.9-56.7); CR 24.1%; PR 24%. Median DoR 10.3 months (13.4 mo for CR, 5.7 mo for PR); median PFS 4.9 months; median OS 9.9 months. Grade >=3 TEAEs: neutropenia 26%, thrombocytopenia 18%, increased GGT 17%. PBD-related toxicities: edema/effusion, liver enzyme elevation, skin/nail changes. In 13 patients with prior CD19 CAR T, ORR was similar to overall - and CD19 expression generally maintained after loncastuximab (antigen loss not common).
grounds_rubric_claims: Loncastuximab tesirine is an approved 3L+ CD19 ADC option for R/R DLBCL, ORR ~48%, CR ~24%
Active on high-risk subgroups (double/triple-hit, transformed, primary-refractory) that other regimens exclude

CD19-directed ADC raises antigen-loss sequencing considerations with CD19 CAR T; biopsy CD19 required after prior CD19 therapy; CD19 expression usually retained, so it does not preclude subsequent CD19 CAR T
Can be used before or after CD19 CAR T (bidirectional sequencing)

verbatim_quotes.quote: 70 of 145 patients had complete or partial response (overall response rate 48.3% [95% CI 39.9-56.7]); 35 had complete response and 35 had partial response
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline efficacy of single-agent loncastuximab tesirine 3L+

verbatim_quotes.quote: a small study of patients who had CD19-directed CAR T-cell therapy after loncastuximab tesirine therapy found CD19 expression after loncastuximab tesirine treatment in all patients with biopsies (ten of 14 patients), suggesting CD19 antigen loss after loncastuximab tesirine is not common.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Antigen-loss sequencing note: loncastuximab (CD19 ADC) generally does not cause CD19 loss precluding subsequent CD19 CAR T

verbatim_quotes.quote: Biopsy-proven CD19 expression was required for patients with previous CD19-directed therapy.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Eligibility rule tied to CD19 antigen status - grounds antigen-loss sequencing rubric consideration

title: Chemoimmunotherapy with methotrexate, cytarabine, thiotepa, and rituximab (MATRix regimen) in patients with primary CNS lymphoma: first randomisation of the IELSG32 phase 2 trial
design: International, multicentre, open-label, randomised phase 2 trial (53 centres, five European countries); first randomisation (1:1:1) of induction: group A methotrexate-cytarabine; group B +rituximab; group C +rituximab+thiotepa (MATRix). Primary endpoint centrally assessed complete remission rate, modified intention-to-treat.
population: 227 enrolled / 219 assessable HIV-negative patients aged 18-70 (median ~57) with newly diagnosed primary CNS lymphoma (all DLBCL histology), disease confined to CNS/cranial nerves/eyes.
line: First line (induction) for newly diagnosed primary CNS lymphoma.
key_eligibility: Age 18-70, histologically proven B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma confined to CNS/cranial nerves/eyes, no prior treatment, measurable disease
ECOG performance status <=3 (or <=2 for ages 66-70)
Excluded: prior organ transplant/immunosuppression, HBV/HCV/HIV infection, other malignancies
outcomes: Complete remission rate 49% (group C, MATRix) vs 30% (group B) vs 23% (group A); MATRix vs methotrexate-cytarabine HR 0.46 (95% CI 0.28-0.74). Overall response 87% vs 74% vs 53%. 2-year progression-free survival 61% (C) vs 46% (B) vs 36% (A); 2-year overall survival 69% (C) vs 56% (B) vs 42% (A). Grade 4 haematological toxicity more frequent with thiotepa but infective complications similar; 13 (6%) died of toxicity during induction. Thiotepa crosses the blood-brain barrier (plasma-to-CSF ratio 100%).
grounds_rubric_claims: MATRix (high-dose methotrexate + cytarabine + thiotepa + rituximab) is the CNS-penetrant standard induction backbone for primary CNS lymphoma in patients up to 70
CNS-directed regimens must use drugs with CNS bioavailability/blood-brain-barrier penetration (methotrexate, cytarabine, thiotepa)
Adding thiotepa and rituximab to methotrexate-cytarabine significantly improves complete remission, PFS, and OS

verbatim_quotes.quote: the IELSG32 trial provides a high level of evidence supporting the use of MATRix combination as the new standard chemoimmunotherapy for patients aged up to 70 years with newly diagnosed primary CNS lymphoma.
verbatim_quotes.supports: MATRix as standard CNS-penetrant induction backbone for PCNSL

verbatim_quotes.quote: patients treated with rituximab and thiotepa had a complete remission rate of 49% (95% CI 38-60), compared with 23% (14-31) of those treated with methotrexate-cytarabine alone (hazard ratio 0.46, 95% CI 0.28-0.74).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline efficacy of MATRix vs methotrexate-cytarabine

verbatim_quotes.quote: Thiotepa is an alkylating agent that is active against aggressive lymphomas, and can cross the blood-brain barrier with a plasma-to-cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) ratio of 100%.
verbatim_quotes.supports: CNS penetration rationale for the regimen components

caveats: Randomised phase 2 (calibration design, no formal pre-planned between-group comparison originally); open-label. Upper age limit 70 and ECOG PS <=3 (<=2 if 66-70), so not directly applicable to older/frailer PCNSL patients. 6% induction toxic-death rate. Consolidation (second randomisation, WBRT vs ASCT) reported separately.

title: Simplified Geriatric Assessment in Older Patients With Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma: The Prospective Elderly Project of the Fondazione Italiana Linfomi
authors_year: Merli F, Luminari S, Tucci A, et al. 2021
design: Prospective multicenter observational study (Elderly Project; NCT02364050) across 36 FIL centers; treatment left to physician discretion. Validates a simplified geriatric assessment (sGA) and builds/validates the Elderly Prognostic Index (EPI). Primary endpoint OS. Externally validated on 328 independent cases.
population: 1,163-1,207 patients with newly diagnosed DLBCL aged >=65 (median age 76, range 65-94). sGA classified 54% fit, 28% unfit, 18% frail. First-line (frontline) elderly DLBCL, not R/R.
line: First-line (newly diagnosed elderly DLBCL). Relevant as a fitness/frailty framework transferable to treatment-intensity decisions across lines.
key_eligibility: Histologically confirmed DLBCL, age >=65 years, signed consent, oGA results available
Excluded: diagnoses other than DLBCL, including follicular lymphoma grade 3b or high-grade lymphoma
sGA defined by age (>= or <80 years) plus CIRS-G comorbidity, ADL, and IADL scores
outcomes: 3-year OS 65% overall. By sGA fitness: fit 75%, unfit 58%, frail 43% (3-year OS). HR unfit vs fit 1.98 (95% CI 1.55-2.54), frail vs fit 3.27 (2.52-4.22). Full-dose anthracycline delivered to 86% of fit, 48% of unfit, 16% of frail. Treatment-related toxicity caused 20.9% of deaths. EPI (integrating sGA + IPI + hemoglobin) stratified low/intermediate/high risk with 3-year OS 87% / 69% / 42%.
grounds_rubric_claims: Frailty/fitness status (functional age), NOT chronologic age, predicts survival and treatment tolerability in older DLBCL: age alone did not independently drive outcome once fitness was accounted for, and in refractory DLBCL (SCHOLAR-1) age >=65 vs <65 did not affect OS
Fit older patients tolerate and benefit from curative-intent (full-dose) therapy, so age alone should not exclude a patient from aggressive/curative treatment
A structured geriatric assessment (fit/unfit/frail) should inform treatment-intensity decisions rather than deferring to subjective clinical judgment or a raw age cutoff
Frail patients have markedly worse survival (3-yr OS 43%) and receive full-dose therapy far less often (16%), supporting attenuated/palliative goals in the frail subgroup

verbatim_quotes.quote: The sGA adopted in our study is an objective, reproducible tool... The time has come to consider the fitness status of older patients with DLBCL to better identify their treatment goals.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Fitness status (not age) should drive treatment goals in older DLBCL.

verbatim_quotes.quote: the OS was significantly different in the three geriatric fitness status groups, with an hazard ratio (HR) of 1.98 for unfit versus fit... 3.27 for frail versus fit... and 1.65 for frail versus unfit.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Frailty status independently predicts survival.

verbatim_quotes.quote: their excellent projected 3-year OS of 87% makes this group of patients suitable for curative approaches similar to those adopted for patients younger than 60 years.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Fit older patients tolerate and warrant curative-intent therapy regardless of chronologic age.

caveats: Frontline, newly diagnosed elderly DLBCL (not R/R) and observational; treatment was physician's discretion, not randomized. HGBCL and FL grade 3b excluded. The fit/unfit/frail construct is transferable to R/R treatment-intensity reasoning but was not derived in the R/R setting.

title: Mosunetuzumab With Polatuzumab Vedotin in Relapsed or Refractory Aggressive Large B-Cell Lymphoma: A Phase 1b/2 Trial
design: Ongoing phase 1b/2 multi-arm trial (NCT03671018); phase 1b dose escalation (n=22) + single-arm phase 2 dose expansion (n=98). Outpatient, fixed-duration mosunetuzumab (step-up dosing, 8-17 cycles) + polatuzumab vedotin 1.8 mg/kg C1-C6. RP2D mosunetuzumab 1/2/60/30 mg.
population: 120 enrolled overall (117 efficacy-evaluable LBCL); dose-expansion 98 R/R LBCL. Median age 68 (range 20-88); median 2 prior lines (range 1-10); 35% prior CAR-T; 12.5% prior ASCT; 57.5% primary refractory; 20.2% double/triple-hit.
line: 2L+ (positioned as second-line, transplant-ineligible; 1-10 prior lines, median 2)
