key_eligibility: R/R aggressive LBCL: DLBCL, HGBCL, transformed FL, grade 3b FL (grade 1-3a FL excluded from efficacy)
Transplant-ineligible second-line and later R/R LBCL
Prior CAR-T permitted and included (35%); primary-refractory patients included (57.5%)
Positioned explicitly as a fixed-duration outpatient regimen
outcomes: Dose-expansion (n=98, RP2D): IRC best ORR 59.2% (95% CI 48.8-69.0); best CR 45.9% (95% CI 35.8-56.3) vs 42% historical control (P=0.0003). Median DoR 20.8 mo; median DoCR not reached (95% CI 20.5-NE); 24-mo CR event-free rate 60.8%. Median PFS 11.4 mo (95% CI 6.2-18.7). Median OS 23.3 mo (95% CI 14.8-NE). Prior-CAR-T subgroup (n=35): ORR 57%, median PFS 9.6 mo. Primary-refractory: median PFS 8.5 mo. Any-grade CRS 16.7% (grade 3 2.5%, no grade 4); ICANS-consistent 5%; grade >=3 neutropenia 25%; no febrile neutropenia; treatment-related discontinuation 10%.
grounds_rubric_claims: Mosun-pola is a fixed-duration, outpatient chemo-free bispecific + ADC regimen suitable for second-line transplant-ineligible R/R LBCL
Active after prior CAR-T (ORR 57% in prior-CAR-T subgroup) and in primary-refractory disease (included; 57.5% of cohort)
Low CRS (16.7%, grade 3 only 2.5%) supports outpatient administration
CR rate (45.9%) exceeds single-agent mosunetuzumab (24%) and single-agent polatuzumab (13%), suggesting combination synergy
Enrolled a higher proportion of primary-refractory (57.5% vs 19%) and post-CAR-T (35% vs 0%) patients than L-MIND tafa-len population

verbatim_quotes.quote: These data demonstrate that mosunetuzumab plus polatuzumab vedotin has a favorable safety profile with highly durable responses suitable as second-line therapy in transplant-ineligible relapsed/refractory LBCL.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Second-line transplant-ineligible positioning / fixed-duration outpatient regimen

verbatim_quotes.quote: Best complete response was 45.9% (95% CI: 35.8-56.3)... Median IRC-assessed PFS was 11.4 months (95% CI: 6.2-18.7). Median overall survival was 23.3 months (95% CI: 14.8-NE).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline efficacy outcomes (CR, PFS, OS)

verbatim_quotes.quote: Notably, the current study enrolled a higher proportion of primary refractory patients (57.5% versus 19.0%) and post-CAR-T cell therapy patients (35% versus 0%) compared to the tafa-len population in the L-MIND study.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Contrast with L-MIND eligibility (primary-refractory and post-CAR-T inclusion)

caveats: Single-arm phase 2 (n=98), no randomized comparator; historical control 42%. Small high-risk subgroups. Grade 1-3a FL excluded from efficacy. Peripheral neuropathy 30.8% (polatuzumab-driven). Applicability limited in patients refractory to prior polatuzumab vedotin.

title: Autologous Bone Marrow Transplantation as Compared with Salvage Chemotherapy in Relapses of Chemotherapy-Sensitive Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma (PARMA)
authors_year: Philip T, Guglielmi C, Hagenbeek A, et al. 1995
design: Prospective, multicenter, randomized trial. All 215 relapsed patients received 2 courses of DHAP salvage chemotherapy; the 109 chemosensitivity-confirmed responders were randomized to high-dose chemotherapy (BEAC) + autologous bone marrow transplantation (± involved-field RT) vs 4 further courses of DHAP (conventional treatment). Primary comparison: event-free and overall survival. The foundational trial establishing ASCT for chemosensitive relapsed aggressive NHL.
population: Adults 18-60 years with intermediate- or high-grade NHL in first (n=188) or second (n=27) relapse, all with prior CR to a doxorubicin-containing first-line regimen. Only chemosensitive relapses (CR/PR to 2 courses of DHAP) were randomized (N=109; transplant 55 vs conventional 54).
line: Relapsed aggressive NHL (historical 2L salvage/ASCT paradigm)
key_eligibility: CHEMOSENSITIVITY IS A PREREQUISITE: only patients responding (CR/PR) to 2 courses of DHAP salvage were randomized. 90 non-responders were excluded before randomization and had markedly worse survival (11% at 5 years).
Required prior doxorubicin (anthracycline)-containing regimen with a documented complete response to first-line therapy.
EXCLUDED patients with CNS or bone marrow involvement at relapse.
Age ceiling ~60 years (ASCT restricted to younger/fit patients).
Establishes that ASCT benefit is confined to chemosensitive disease — chemorefractory relapse does not benefit and needs an alternative (now CAR-T).
outcomes: At median 63-month follow-up: 5-year event-free survival 46% (transplant) vs 12% (conventional), P=0.001; 5-year overall survival 53% vs 32%, P=0.038. Response rate 84% (ASCT) vs 44% (chemo). Chemorefractory (DHAP-nonresponder) patients had only 11% 5-year survival (P<0.001 vs randomized). Toxic-death rate 6% in transplant arm. Establishes salvage-chemo-then-ASCT as standard for chemosensitive relapsed aggressive NHL.
grounds_rubric_claims: ASCT for relapsed aggressive B-cell lymphoma requires demonstrated chemosensitivity (response to salvage chemotherapy) as a prerequisite
Chemorefractory relapse does NOT benefit from ASCT and needs an alternative modality (the gap CAR-T fills)
Historical standard of care for chemosensitive relapse = salvage chemoimmunotherapy followed by high-dose therapy + autologous transplant (the SOC comparator arm in ZUMA-7/BELINDA/TRANSFORM)

verbatim_quotes.quote: Patients with complete or partial responses were considered to have relapses of chemotherapy-sensitive lymphoma and were eligible for random assignment to one of the treatment groups.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Chemosensitivity (response to salvage DHAP) is the gating eligibility criterion for ASCT randomization

verbatim_quotes.quote: At five years, the rate of event-free survival was 46 percent in the transplantation group and 12 percent in the group receiving chemotherapy without transplantation (P = 0.001), and the rate of overall survival was 53 and 32 percent, respectively (P = 0.038).
verbatim_quotes.supports: ASCT improves EFS and OS over salvage chemotherapy in chemosensitive relapsed aggressive NHL

verbatim_quotes.quote: the overall survival at five years in the group of 109 randomized patients was 42 percent... and 11 percent in the group of 90 patients without initial responses to chemotherapy (P < 0.001).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Chemorefractory (salvage-nonresponsive) patients derive minimal benefit — ASCT is not appropriate without chemosensitivity

caveats: 1995 trial predating rituximab, PET staging, and CAR-T; conducted with autologous bone marrow (not peripheral blood stem cells) and included some indolent histologies later reclassified. Its enduring rubric relevance is the principle that ASCT requires chemosensitivity — CORAL and later data show <12-month/refractory relapse responds poorly to salvage, which is precisely the population now directed to 2L CAR-T per ZUMA-7.

title: Polatuzumab Vedotin in Relapsed or Refractory Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma
design: International, multicenter, open-label phase Ib/II trial (GO29365; NCT02257567). Randomised cohort (n=80; 40 per arm) comparing polatuzumab vedotin + bendamustine + rituximab (pola-BR) vs BR alone; plus single-arm pola-BG cohort (n=27). Primary endpoint: IRC-assessed CR rate at end of treatment. Up to six 21-day cycles.
population: Adults with transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL (excluding transformed lymphoma) after >=1 prior line; ECOG 0-2; grade <=1 peripheral neuropathy. Median 2 prior lines; most refractory to last therapy (75% pola-BR, 85% BR); median age 67-71. Double/triple-hit not excluded.
line: Approved third-line or later (3L+); trial enrolled >=1 prior line (transplant-ineligible).
key_eligibility: Transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL, or prior ASCT failure
Transformed lymphoma EXCLUDED; peripheral neuropathy must be grade <=1 at entry
Contains bendamustine - relevant to sequencing hazard before cellular therapy (see BENDA-PREAPHERESIS)
outcomes: Randomised cohort: IRC CR rate 40.0% (pola-BR) vs 17.5% (BR); P=0.026. Median IRC PFS 9.5 vs 3.7 months (HR 0.36; 95% CI 0.21-0.63; P<0.001). Median OS 12.4 vs 4.7 months (HR 0.42; 95% CI 0.24-0.75; P=0.002) - 58% reduction in risk of death. Higher grade 3-4 neutropenia (46.2% vs 33.3%), anaemia, and thrombocytopenia with pola-BR; grade 1-2 polatuzumab-associated peripheral neuropathy in 43.6%, mostly resolving.
grounds_rubric_claims: Pola-BR is an approved 3L+ regimen for transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL with OS benefit over BR (HR 0.42)
Regimen contains bendamustine - flags a sequencing hazard when cellular (CAR T) therapy may follow, since recent bendamustine before apheresis worsens CAR T outcomes
Pola-BR excludes transformed lymphoma in its pivotal evidence (eligibility caveat)
caveats: Small randomised cohort (40/arm), phase Ib/II. Bendamustine backbone raises concern if CAR T is a subsequent planned therapy.

verbatim_quotes.quote: pola-BR patients had a significantly higher IRC-assessed CR rate (40.0% v 17.5%; P = .026) and longer IRC-assessed PFS (median, 9.5 v 3.7 months; ... P < .001) and OS (median, 12.4 v 4.7 months; HR, 0.42; 95% CI, 0.24 to 0.75; P = .002...).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Efficacy of pola-BR over BR: higher CR, longer PFS and OS in transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL

verbatim_quotes.quote: Polatuzumab vedotin combined with BR resulted in a significantly higher CR rate and reduced the risk of death by 58% compared with BR in patients with transplantation-ineligible R/R DLBCL.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline conclusion; grounds approved-3L+ role and 58% OS risk reduction

verbatim_quotes.quote: All patients received bendamustine 90 mg/m2 intravenously
verbatim_quotes.supports: Regimen contains bendamustine - grounds the sequencing-hazard link to impaired CAR T apheresis/outcomes

title: Attenuated immunochemotherapy regimen (R-miniCHOP) in elderly patients older than 80 years with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma: a multicentre, single-arm, phase 2 trial
design: Prospective, multicentre, single-arm, phase 2 trial (38 centres, France and Belgium); 6 cycles of R-miniCHOP every 3 weeks; primary endpoint overall survival, intention-to-treat.
population: 149 patients (of 150 enrolled) aged over 80 years (median 83, range 80-95) with untreated CD20+ DLBCL; 75% Ann Arbor stage III/IV; ECOG PS <=2.
line: First line (untreated DLBCL) in patients >=80 years.
key_eligibility: Age over 80 with untreated histologically proven CD20+ DLBCL (WHO classification)
Ann Arbor stage I bulky to IV; age-adjusted IPI 1-3; ECOG PS <=2; life expectancy >=3 months

Excluded: CNS or meningeal involvement, other lymphoma subtypes, history of indolent lymphoma, poor renal (creatinine >150 umol/L) or hepatic function, poor marrow reserve, prior cancer within 5 years, patients under guardianship
outcomes: Median overall survival 29 months; 2-year OS 59% (95% CI 49-67). Median PFS 21 months; 2-year PFS 47% (38-56). Overall response rate 73%; complete/unconfirmed-complete response rate 62%. On multivariate analysis only serum albumin <=35 g/L predicted worse OS (HR 3.2, 95% CI 1.4-7.1, p=0.0053). 58 deaths (33 from lymphoma progression, 12 attributed to treatment toxicity). Grade >=3 neutropenia in 59 patients; febrile neutropenia in 11. Doses: rituximab 375 mg/m2, cyclophosphamide 400 mg/m2, doxorubicin 25 mg/m2, vincristine 1 mg (all day 1), prednisone 40 mg/m2 days 1-5.
grounds_rubric_claims: R-miniCHOP is a validated, prospectively studied first-line regimen for DLBCL in patients >=80 with good performance status; attenuated dosing is intentional and evidence-based, not inappropriate under-dosing
Curative-intent immunochemotherapy is achievable in the very elderly (2-year OS 59%, CR/CRu 62%)
Serum albumin <=35 g/L (a nutrition/frailty marker) is the dominant adverse prognostic factor in this population

verbatim_quotes.quote: R-miniCHOP offers a good compromise between efficacy and safety in patients aged over 80 years old. R-miniCHOP should be considered as the new standard treatment in this subgroup of patients.
verbatim_quotes.supports: R-miniCHOP is the standard first-line regimen for DLBCL patients >80, validating attenuated dosing

verbatim_quotes.quote: the median overall survival was 29 months (95% CI 21 to upper limit not reached); 2-year overall survival was 59% (49-67%).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline efficacy outcomes

verbatim_quotes.quote: In multivariate analyses, overall survival was only affected by a serum albumin concentration of 35 g/L or less (hazard ratio 3.2, 95% CI 1.4-7.1; p=0.0053).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Low serum albumin as the key adverse prognostic factor

caveats: Single-arm, no control group; selected patients (ECOG PS <=2, adequate organ/marrow function) so not generalizable to frail/unfit >=80. 12 toxic deaths (>20% of deaths); most grade 3-4 hematologic toxicity and treatment-related deaths clustered in cycle 1. No formal comprehensive geriatric assessment performed (only IADL).

title: Toxicity and efficacy of CAR T-cell therapy in patients with DLBCL above the age of 70 years compared to younger patients - a matched control multicenter cohort study
design: Retrospective matched-control multicenter cohort (3 Israeli centers); elderly (>=70) vs younger (<70) matched on ECOG PS (0-1 vs 2-3) and LDH (high vs normal)
population: R/R DLBCL. 47 patients >=70 underwent apheresis; 41 infused (mean age 76.2) = study cohort, matched to 41 younger (mean 55.4). Commercial tisa-cel (~80%) or axi-cel (~20%). Non-selective real-world referral: 66% poor PS (ECOG 2-3), ~40% high LDH, ~48% progressive disease pre-infusion, ~47% >=3 prior lines.
key_eligibility: Consecutive R/R DLBCL patients >=70 years referred for CD19 CAR-T (study cohort), matched to <70 controls
No centralized eligibility committee; national infrastructure did not mandate strict PS/organ cutoffs — enabling a non-selective elderly population
Only exclusions at screening: ECOG 4 (n=1) and active hepatitis B (n=1); 96% of referred elderly were eligible and apheresed
Explicitly NO numeric age cap — age itself not an exclusion
outcomes: 41/47 (87%) infused; 96% of referred elderly eligible for apheresis. Response similar: CR 46% + PR 17% (ORR 63%) elderly vs 78% younger (P=0.337). Toxicity comparable: grade >=3 CRS 9.8% vs 7.3% (P=0.29); grade 3-4 ICANS 2.5% vs 4.9% (P=0.54); overall ICANS 27.5% vs 17.1% (P=0.48); hospital stay 23.4 vs 24.6 days (P=0.55). Non-relapse mortality 0 at 1 and 3 months both groups. Elderly 6-/12-mo PFS 39%/32%; OS 74%/69%. Age and ECOG PS had NO impact on CR, PFS, or OS in multivariate models; only high LDH, non-GCB, bridging, and post-CAR CR state mattered.
grounds_rubric_claims: CAR-T is feasible and effective in selected older adults (>=70); age per se should not preclude CAR-T
No numeric age cutoff is justified — outcomes matched younger patients when controlled for PS and LDH
Logistics/fitness composite (rapid referral-to-infusion, lymphocyte fitness, LDH, PS) drives outcome more than chronological age
ICANS severity was low here (tisa-cel-predominant); toxicity not increased by age in this cohort, but authors note axi-cel series report higher ICANS in older patients
Prior vascular disease and dementia did NOT predict ICANS in this cohort

verbatim_quotes.quote: outcomes of CAR-T cell therapy are comparable between elderly, geriatric and younger patients, indicating that age as per se should not preclude CAR-T cell administration.
verbatim_quotes.supports: No numeric age cutoff; age alone should not exclude CAR-T

verbatim_quotes.quote: There was no difference between the elderly and the control group in overall ICANS (27.5% vs. 17.1%. respectively, P=0.48) and grade 3-4 ICANS (2.5% vs. 4.9%, respectively, P=0.54).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Toxicity (incl. neurotoxicity) not increased by age in this tisa-cel-predominant cohort

verbatim_quotes.quote: Ideally, a geriatric scoring assessment should be employed and help to establish eligibility criteria for CAR-T cell therapy in this aged group pf patients.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Fitness/geriatric assessment (not age) should govern selection

caveats: Small (n=41 per arm), retrospective, single-country. Predominantly tisagenlecleucel, which the authors note carries lower ICANS than axi-cel — so the flat ICANS-by-age finding may not generalize to axi-cel. Possible residual selection: presumably ineligible patients may never have been referred. Matched on ECOG/LDH only; imbalance in ECOG 3 (12% elderly vs 39% younger) and prior autoHCT.

title: Lisocabtagene maraleucel as second-line therapy in adults with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma who were not intended for haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (PILOT)
authors_year: Sehgal A, Hoda D, Riedell PA, et al. 2022
design: Open-label, single-arm, phase 2 trial (PILOT; NCT03483103) at 18 US sites. Primary endpoint ORR by IRC (Lugano 2014). Null hypothesis ORR <=50.2% from a real-world synthetic control.
population: 61 patients treated (efficacy/safety set; 74 leukapheresed). R/R LBCL (DLBCL NOS, transformed FL, HGBCL double/triple-hit, FL grade 3B). Median age 74 (IQR 70-78), 46% aged >=75; 26% ECOG PS 2; 44% HCT-CI >=3. Disease status: 54% refractory, 21% relapsed <=12 mo, 25% relapsed >12 mo. 52% received bridging therapy.
line: Second-line (exactly one prior line of anthracycline + CD20-containing chemoimmunotherapy), in patients NOT intended for HSCT.
key_eligibility: Relapsed or refractory (< CR to first-line) LBCL after exactly ONE prior line containing an anthracycline and a CD20-targeted agent

NOT intended for HSCT by the investigator (subjective) AND met >=1 of 6 objective transplantation-not-intended (TNI) criteria: age >=70, ECOG PS 2, DLCO <=60%, LVEF <50%, CrCl <60 mL/min, or AST/ALT >2x ULN
PET-positive disease; adequate organ function for CAR T-cell therapy
Secondary CNS involvement was allowed
outcomes: ORR 80% (49/61; 95% CI 68-89; p<0.0001) and CR rate 54% (33/61; 95% CI 41-67). Median PFS 9.03 mo overall (22.60 mo in CR patients); median OS not reached; median DOR 12.09 mo overall and 21.65 mo in CR patients. CRS in 38% (23/61), grade 3 in only 1 (2%), no grade 4-5. Neurological events in 31% (grade 3 in 3 [5%], no grade 4-5). Grade >=3 prolonged cytopenia at day 28 in 30%. No CRS/neuro events led to treatment discontinuation; no treatment-related deaths in the CAR-T causal sense (2 COVID-19 deaths).
grounds_rubric_claims: Liso-cel is an evidence-supported 2L option for transplant-INELIGIBLE R/R LBCL regardless of relapse timing (works even in patients who relapse >12 months, who are ineligible for the 2L CAR-T trials TRANSFORM/ZUMA-7 that required refractory/early-relapse)
Transplant-ineligibility by objective TNI criteria (age >=70, ECOG 2, organ dysfunction) qualifies a patient for 2L CAR-T even though they are not fit for HSCT
CAR-T is feasible and tolerable in an older, comorbid population (median age 74, 46% >=75, 44% HCT-CI >=3) with lower/manageable CRS (grade 3 only 2%) and ICANS (grade 3 5%)

verbatim_quotes.quote: These results support lisocabtagene maraleucel as a potential second-line treatment in patients with large B-cell lymphoma for whom HSCT is not intended.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Grounds liso-cel as a 2L option specifically for the transplant-ineligible population.

verbatim_quotes.quote: 33 (54%) had refractory disease, 13 (21%) relapsed within 1 year of first-line therapy, and 15 (25%) relapsed after 12 months of first-line therapy.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Includes patients relapsing >12 months, so 2L liso-cel applies regardless of relapse timing when transplant-ineligible.

verbatim_quotes.quote: Cytokine release syndrome occurred in 23 (38%; grade 3 in one) patients and neurological events in 19 (31%; grade 3 in three) patients, with no grade 4 events or deaths.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Lower/manageable CRS and ICANS in this older transplant-ineligible cohort.

caveats: Single-arm, synthetic (real-world) control for the null hypothesis. Older/comorbid population by design; 52% received bridging therapy (baseline SPD post-bridging understates tumor burden). Median on-study follow-up 12.3 mo.

title: Safety and efficacy of CAR T-cell therapy in central nervous system lymphoma: a systematic review and meta-analysis
design: Systematic review and meta-analysis (PRISMA 2020; PROSPERO CRD420251070033) of 39 studies (38 in quantitative analysis; up to 1,190 patients). Predominantly retrospective single-arm cohorts (n=28) plus 11 single-arm interventional/phase 1-2 studies; NO randomized comparisons. Freeman-Tukey double-arcsine pooling, random-effects (DerSimonian-Laird).
population: Adults (>=18) with PCNSL or SCNSL treated with CD19-based CAR T-cell therapy (axi-cel, tisa-cel, liso-cel, brexu-cel, and dual/triple-targeted constructs). 20 studies SCNSL, 5 PCNSL, 13 both. Predominant histology DLBCL; includes double-/triple-hit subsets in several cohorts. No restriction on prior lines or bridging.
key_eligibility: Included adults with PCNSL or SCNSL (systemic NHL with CNS involvement) receiving CD19-based CAR-T; single- and dual/triple-targeted constructs allowed
Case reports with <3 patients, reviews, editorials, and preclinical studies EXCLUDED; required separately reported CNS-lymphoma outcomes
Both prospective and retrospective designs, including case series with >=3 patients; no restriction on prior therapy lines, bridging, or geography
Rationale: pivotal CAR-T trials systematically excluded active CNS involvement due to neurotoxicity concerns
outcomes: Pooled ORR 0.75 (95% CI 0.70-0.79; I2=53.3%); CR 0.52 (0.46-0.58); PR 0.18 (0.14-0.22). No significant difference in ORR between PCNSL and SCNSL (p=0.063). PFS pooled HR PCNSL vs SCNSL 0.89 (95% CI 0.16-4.91, NS). OS rate 0.71 at 6 mo to 0.48 at 24 mo; PFS 0.41 at 6 mo to 0.28 at 24 mo. Pooled median-of-medians OS 8.6 mo, PFS 3.6 mo. CRS 83.5% (grade>=3 5.77%); ICANS 44.9% (grade>=3 17.4%).
grounds_rubric_claims: Meta-analytic (non-randomized) evidence supports CAR T-cell therapy achieving robust responses in CNS lymphoma (ORR ~75%, CR ~52%)
Efficacy of CAR-T is comparable between primary and secondary CNS lymphoma
Severe CRS is infrequent (grade>=3 ~6%) and severe ICANS is manageable (~17%) in CNS-lymphoma CAR-T
Evidence base for CAR-T in CNS lymphoma is non-randomized (retrospective cohorts + single-arm trials), as CNS involvement was excluded from pivotal trials

verbatim_quotes.quote: The pooled overall response rate (ORR) was 0.75 [95% CI: 0.70-0.79]... Complete response (CR) rate was 0.52 [0.46-0.58]
verbatim_quotes.supports: Pooled CAR-T efficacy in CNS lymphoma: ORR 75%, CR 52%

verbatim_quotes.quote: CART therapy achieves robust response rates in CNS lymphoma, with efficacy comparable between PCNSL and SCNSL. Neurotoxicity remains frequent but manageable, and severe CRS events are infrequent.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Overall conclusion: comparable PCNSL/SCNSL efficacy; manageable neurotoxicity; infrequent severe CRS

verbatim_quotes.quote: Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS) occurred in 83.5% [79.0-88.0], with grade>=3 CRS in 5.77% [3.0-9.0]. Immune Effector Cell-Associated Neurotoxicity Syndrome (ICANS) was reported in 44.9% [36.0-54.0], with severe (grade >=3) events in 17.4% [12.0-23.0].
verbatim_quotes.supports: Safety profile: CRS 83.5% (severe 5.8%), ICANS 44.9% (severe 17.4%)

caveats: Non-randomized evidence: 28/39 studies retrospective single-arm; substantial heterogeneity (ICANS I2=84%). PFS HR comparison PCNSL vs SCNSL very wide (0.16-4.91). Pooled proportions across heterogeneous products, prior-line burden, and bridging. Registry/abstract data included.

title: Use of comprehensive geriatric assessment in older cancer patients: recommendations from the SIOG task force on CGA
authors_year: Extermann, Aapro, Bernabei, Cohen, Droz, Lichtman, Mor, Monfardini, Repetto, Sorbye, Topinkova, 2005
design: Systematic evidence review and consensus recommendations (adapted Oxford levels of evidence) from an eight-member SIOG task force addressing four key questions about CGA in older cancer patients.
population: Older cancer patients (soft age limit ~70); evidence base drawn largely from community-dwelling elderly and geriatric RCTs, with limited cancer-specific data.
line: N/A (assessment/care-process recommendation).
key_eligibility: Recommends screening for geriatric problems in cancer patients beyond age 70 (a 'soft limit')
Systematic review of Medline literature through February 2003 rated by adapted Oxford criteria
outcomes: Concludes there is strong evidence that CGA detects problems missed by routine assessment and that CGA improves function and reduces hospitalization in the elderly; heterogeneous evidence for survival and cost-effectiveness; corroborative but limited cancer-specific evidence. Recommends that functional assessment be more extensive than ECOG performance status (adding ADL/IADL and a performance test such as timed get-up-and-go), plus screening for depression and cognition, and that CGA include follow-up. Cannot recommend any single tool above others.
grounds_rubric_claims: Comprehensive geriatric assessment should be used in older cancer patients to detect problems missed by standard oncologic assessment
Functional assessment should go beyond ECOG performance status (ADL/IADL, performance measures)
Geriatric screening is recommended beyond age ~70 as a soft limit

verbatim_quotes.quote: A CGA, with or without screening, and with follow-up, should be used in older cancer patients, in order to detect unaddressed problems, improve their functional status, and possibly their survival.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Core SIOG recommendation to use CGA in older cancer patients

verbatim_quotes.quote: functional status assessment should be more extensive than the ECOG performance status evaluation and may include ADL/IADL, and/or a performance test such as the timed get up and go.
verbatim_quotes.supports: CGA/GA should extend beyond ECOG performance status

verbatim_quotes.quote: Based on retrospective evidence that the incidence of geriatric problems increases sharply after 70 in cancer patients, we recommend screening for them beyond that age. This should be considered a soft limit.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Age ~70 soft threshold for geriatric screening

caveats: Explicitly labeled 'recommendations' not guidelines; evidence for specific CGA components is weak and largely extrapolated from non-cancer geriatric trials. No single tool endorsed. Publications only through early 2003.

title: Glofitamab plus gemcitabine and oxaliplatin (GemOx) versus rituximab-GemOx for relapsed or refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (STARGLO): a global phase 3, randomised, open-label trial
design: Global phase 3, randomised, open-label trial; 62 centres in 13 countries; NCT04408638. Randomised 2:1 to Glofit-GemOx (n=183) vs R-GemOx (n=91). Primary endpoint overall survival. Primary analysis cutoff Mar 2023; updated analysis Feb 2024.
population: Transplant-INELIGIBLE adults with histologically confirmed R/R DLBCL NOS after >=1 prior therapy. Median age 68; median 1 prior line (37% had >=2). Patients with 1 prior line had to be ASCT-ineligible (age >=70, end-organ dysfunction, ECOG >=2, refusal, comorbidity). NOTE: transformed, double/triple-hit, and HGBCL NOS were EXCLUDED.
line: Second-line or later in transplant-ineligible patients (>=1 prior therapy).
key_eligibility: Transplant-ineligible only; if only one prior line, must be ASCT-ineligible by defined criteria (age >=70, end-organ dysfunction, ECOG >=2, refusal, comorbidity)
EXCLUDED: DLBCL transformed from indolent lymphoma, double/triple-hit (HGBCL with MYC + BCL2/BCL6), and HGBCL NOS - restricts to DLBCL NOS
Prior CAR T-cell therapy allowed (8% had prior CAR T)
outcomes: Updated analysis (median f/u 20.7 mo): median OS 25.5 months (Glofit-GemOx) vs 12.9 months (R-GemOx); HR 0.62 (95% CI 0.43-0.88). Primary analysis OS HR 0.59 (0.40-0.89; p=0.011). IRC PFS 13.8 vs 3.6 months (HR 0.40). IRC CR rate 58.5% vs 25.3%; ORR 68.3% vs 40.7%. CRS in 44% of glofitamab-exposed (predominantly low grade; grade >=3 ~2%). REGULATORY NOTE: received US Complete Response Letter / not FDA-approved (per project framing).
grounds_rubric_claims: Glofit-GemOx improves OS over R-GemOx in transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL (2L+), HR ~0.62
Off-the-shelf CD20xCD3 bispecific + chemo is an option for transplant-ineligible patients without CAR T access
Glofit-GemOx not FDA-approved (US CRL) - availability caveat for US practice despite positive phase 3
Excludes transformed / double-hit / HGBCL, so evidence applies to DLBCL NOS transplant-ineligible only
caveats: US regulatory status: received a Complete Response Letter; not FDA-approved (per task framing). Subgroup OS inconsistencies by region/race noted; NALT censoring imbalance prompted post-hoc EFS analysis.

verbatim_quotes.quote: At the updated analysis after a median follow-up of 20.7 months (19.9-23.3), a consistent improvement in overall survival was observed with Glofit-GemOx versus R-GemOx (median 25.5 months [18.3-NE] vs 12.9 months [7.9-18.5]; HR 0.62 [0.43-0.88]).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline OS benefit of Glofit-GemOx over R-GemOx in transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL

verbatim_quotes.quote: We recruited transplant-ineligible patients (aged >=18 years) with histologically confirmed relapsed or refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma after one or more previous therapies.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Population is transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL, 2L+

verbatim_quotes.quote: Patients with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma transformed from indolent lymphoma, double or triple hit lymphomas ... or high-grade B-cell lymphoma, not otherwise specified, were not eligible.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Key exclusions restricting evidence to DLBCL NOS (grounds a sequencing/eligibility rubric caveat)

title: Mosunetuzumab Plus Polatuzumab Vedotin in Transplant-Ineligible Refractory/Relapsed Large B-Cell Lymphoma: Primary Results of the Phase III SUNMO Trial
design: Global, randomised, phase 3 trial; 50 sites in 13 countries; NCT05171647. Randomised 2:1 to Mosun-Pola (n=138) vs R-GemOx (n=70). Dual primary endpoints: centrally assessed ORR and PFS; OS key secondary. Median follow-up 23.2 months.
population: Adults with R/R LBCL (DLBCL NOS, HGBCL including double/triple-hit, FL grade 3b, transformed FL) refractory/relapsed after >=1 prior therapy, deemed ASCT-INELIGIBLE by treating physician. Median age 62; 41% >=65; median 2 prior lines (44% had only 1); 74% primary refractory or relapse within 12 months of first-line. Prior polatuzumab permitted if no PD within 12 months of last dose.
line: Second-line or later in transplant-ineligible patients (>=1 prior therapy).
key_eligibility: ASCT-ineligible per treating physician
Previous polatuzumab vedotin permitted only if no disease progression within 12 months of last dose
HGBCL including double-/triple-hit and transformed FL included (broader histology than STARGLO)
Fully outpatient, chemotherapy-free bispecific + ADC regimen; no hospitalization required for mosunetuzumab
outcomes: Median PFS 11.5 months (Mosun-Pola) vs 3.8 months (R-GemOx); HR 0.41 (95% CI 0.3-0.6); P<0.0001. ORR 70% vs 40% (P<0.0001); CR 51% vs 24%. Interim OS 18.7 vs 13.6 months (HR 0.80; P=0.28, not yet significant). CRS in 26% of Mosun-Pola patients; grade >=2 CRS and tocilizumab use each <5%; only one grade 3 CRS, no grade 4/5, no ICANS. Lowest reported CRS among T-cell engagers. Improved patient-reported outcomes and fewer cytopenias/neuropathy vs R-GemOx.
grounds_rubric_claims: Mosun-Pola is a gentler, outpatient, chemo-free option for transplant-ineligible R/R LBCL, superior PFS/ORR vs R-GemOx
Lowest CRS of T-cell engagers (grade >=2 <5%, no ICANS) - preferred where CRS/neurotoxicity risk or CAR-T ineligibility is a concern
Prior polatuzumab within 12 months with PD is an exclusion/sequencing consideration (pola re-exposure limited)
Appropriate for CAR-T-ineligible R/R DLBCL and proposed to replace GemOx as control arm

verbatim_quotes.quote: the median PFS was significantly longer with Mosun-Pola than with R-GemOx (11.5 months [95% CI, 5.6 to 18] v 3.8 months [95% CI, 2.9 to 4.1]; hazard ratio for progression or death, 0.41 [95% CI, 0.3 to 0.6]; P < .0001).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Primary PFS benefit of Mosun-Pola over R-GemOx in transplant-ineligible R/R LBCL

verbatim_quotes.quote: the rate of grade >=2 cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and usage of tocilizumab occurred in <5% of patients
verbatim_quotes.supports: Very low CRS - grounds gentler-option rubric claim for transplant-ineligible patients

verbatim_quotes.quote: Previous treatment with polatuzumab vedotin was permitted if no disease progression (PD) occurred within 12 months of the last dose.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Sequencing constraint on prior polatuzumab exposure (eligibility rule)

title: Tafasitamab for Patients With Relapsed or Refractory DLBCL: Final 5-Year Efficacy and Safety (L-MIND)
design: Open-label, single-arm, global multicenter phase 2 study (NCT02399085). Final 5-year analysis. Tafasitamab + lenalidomide for up to 12 cycles, then tafasitamab monotherapy until progression. Sponsor MorphoSys AG.
population: 80 patients (full analysis set) with ASCT-ineligible R/R DLBCL, 1-3 prior systemic therapies. Median age 72 (range 41-86); 50% had 1 prior line, 50% had >=2; 50% IPI 3-5; 18.8% primary refractory (by amended definition); 11.3% prior ASCT.
line: 2L+ (ASCT-ineligible; 1-3 prior lines; label positions as second-line for transplant-ineligible)
key_eligibility: ASCT-ineligible R/R DLBCL, aged >=18
1 to 3 prior systemic therapies including >=1 CD20-targeting agent
ECOG 0-2

Primary-refractory disease was EXCLUDED per protocol; definition changed during trial (initially no response/progression within 3 mo, amended June 2016 to within 6 mo of prior anti-CD20 regimen), so some early-relapsing patients (3-6 mo) were enrolled -- 18.8% ultimately had primary-refractory disease by the updated definition
US label: DLBCL NOS including DLBCL arising from low-grade lymphoma, ineligible for ASCT
outcomes: Best ORR 57.5% (46/80; 95% CI 45.9-68.5); CR rate 41.3% (n=33); PR 16.3%. Median DoR not reached (median follow-up 44.0 mo); plateau after ~12 mo. Median PFS 11.6 mo (95% CI 5.7-45.7). Median OS 33.5 mo (95% CI 18.3-NR). By prior lines: 1 prior line ORR 67.5% (CR 52.5%), median PFS 23.5 mo; >=2 prior lines ORR 47.5% (CR 30.0%), median PFS 7.6 mo. 5-yr OS 72.7% for best-response CR vs 18.3% for PR. Grade >=3 TEAE 64.2%; neutropenia grade >=3 48.1%. AE frequency decreased on tafasitamab monotherapy.
grounds_rubric_claims: Tafa-len (L-MIND) is a standard second-line option specifically for ASCT-INELIGIBLE R/R DLBCL
Primary-refractory disease was an EXCLUSION criterion in L-MIND (key rubric gate: tafa-len not validated for primary-refractory patients)
Efficacy is markedly higher at 2L (1 prior line: ORR 67.5%, CR 52.5%) than at 3L+ (>=2 prior lines: ORR 47.5%, CR 30%) -- favors earlier use
Restricted to 1-3 prior lines; not studied beyond 3 prior lines
Depth of response (CR) drives long-term survival
Real-world data show 91% of typical R/R LBCL patients would not have met L-MIND eligibility

verbatim_quotes.quote: Patients with primary refractory disease were excluded, but because the definition changed while the study was active, some were eligible and included.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Primary-refractory exclusion criterion (core rubric gate) and the definitional nuance

verbatim_quotes.quote: tafasitamab, an anti-CD19 immunotherapy, is used with lenalidomide for patients with autologous stem cell transplant-ineligible relapsed/refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma
verbatim_quotes.supports: ASCT-ineligible positioning / indication

verbatim_quotes.quote: Patients who had received only one prior line of therapy had a higher ORR (67.5%, with 52.5% CR and 15.0% PR) compared to patients who had received two or more prior lines of therapy (47.5%, with 30.0% CR and 17.5% PR).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Line-of-therapy dependence favoring earlier (2L) use

caveats: Single-arm, small (n=80), no comparator. Primary-refractory definition changed mid-trial, so a minority of primary-refractory (3-6 mo relapse) patients were included. No/minimal prior CAR-T or prior anti-CD19 exposure in cohort; poorly generalizable to post-CAR-T setting (real-world: 91% ineligible for L-MIND).

title: A case of successful CAR-T cell therapy for early isolated CNS recurrence of DLBCL with persistent CAR-T cells
design: Single case report (n=1), non-randomized.
population: 66-year-old man with non-GCB DLBCL (Ann Arbor stage IV; IPI 2; CNS-IPI intermediate). Tumor immunophenotype CD10-, non-GCB (CD10-/BCL6-/MUM1+); positive for BCL2 and MYC by IHC. Achieved CR after 6x R-CHOP, then isolated CNS relapse (leptomeningeal, CSF B-cells 51/uL) 3 months later. Received 7 courses high-dose methotrexate + intrathecal MTX (partial response), then liso-cel CAR-T as second-line.
key_eligibility: Isolated secondary CNS relapse of DLBCL (leptomeningeal/CSF disease; no systemic or radiographic parenchymal disease)
Early relapse (<1 year) after R-CHOP - rationale that ASCT would give only temporary benefit, favoring CAR-T
Liso-cel used as approved second-line therapy for early relapsed/refractory large B-cell lymphoma; tumor MYC+/BCL2+ by IHC (not stated as FISH-confirmed double-hit)
outcomes: Second CR achieved after liso-cel; CSF CR by day 18 with resolution of CNS symptoms, ongoing CR to day 120. CRS grade 2 (treated with tocilizumab); NO ICANS. Prolonged pancytopenia (bone marrow failure) recovered with G-CSF + eltrombopag. CAR-T dynamics: peripheral blood CAR-T 25% (d11), 8% (d18), 0.3% (d90); 46.3% CAR-T detected in CSF on day 18 - demonstrating CAR-T trafficking to meningeal disease. Cites Cook meta-analysis (128 pts; ORR 64% overall, 57% SCNSL; ICANS 48%, grade 3-4 26%).
grounds_rubric_claims: CAR T-cell therapy (liso-cel) can be effective and tolerable in isolated secondary CNS DLBCL relapse (single-case, non-randomized evidence)
Secondary CNS lymphoma requires CNS-penetrant drugs; HD-MTX (with intrathecal MTX) is used before/around CAR-T
CAR-T cells traffic across the blood-brain barrier into CSF/meningeal disease (46.3% CAR-T in CSF at day 18)
In early (<1 yr) post-R-CHOP CNS relapse, CAR-T may be preferable to ASCT (which offers only temporary benefit)
CRS can be manageable (grade 2, tocilizumab) with absent ICANS even in CNS disease

verbatim_quotes.quote: This case is the first to validate the efficacy and safety of CAR-T cell therapy for isolated secondary CNS lymphoma in clinical practice.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Single-case evidence for CAR-T efficacy/safety in isolated secondary CNS DLBCL

verbatim_quotes.quote: we detected 46.3% of CAR-T cells in the CSF (2/uL) on day 18, suggesting that these CAR-T cells can migrate directly to meningeal lesions of DLBCL
verbatim_quotes.supports: CAR-T cells traffic into CSF/meningeal CNS disease

verbatim_quotes.quote: Secondary central nervous system (CNS) lymphomas typically require CNS-penetrating drugs; however, the available agents are limited with temporary effects and poor outcomes.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Rationale for CNS-penetrant drugs in secondary CNS lymphoma and unmet need motivating CAR-T

caveats: n=1 case report - lowest level of evidence, non-randomized, no comparator, short follow-up (CR to ~day 120). MYC/BCL2 were positive by immunohistochemistry only (double-expressor); FISH double-hit status not reported. OCR-garbled source text but clinical facts are legible.

title: Lisocabtagene maraleucel for patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphomas (TRANSCEND NHL 001): a multicentre seamless design study
design: Multicentre, multicohort, open-label, seamless-design phase 1 study of liso-cel (CD19 CAR T) at 14 US cancer centres; NCT02631044. Three target dose levels; efficacy-evaluable set n=256, safety set n=269, ITT (leukapheresed) n=344.
population: Adults with relapsed/refractory LBCL after >=2 prior lines (including anti-CD20 + anthracycline). Histologies: DLBCL NOS, DLBCL transformed from indolent lymphoma, high-grade B-cell lymphoma (double/triple-hit), PMBCL, FL grade 3B. Median 3 prior lines (range 1-8); 67% chemorefractory; 42% aged >=65; heavily pretreated.
line: Third-line or later (3L+); >=2 prior systemic therapies required.
key_eligibility: Secondary CNS lymphoma explicitly ALLOWED/eligible (7 [3%] had secondary CNS involvement) - grounds permissive CNS rule for liso-cel 3L+
Moderate comorbidities permitted: creatinine clearance >30 to <60 mL/min and LVEF >=40% to <50%; no minimum absolute lymphocyte count
Bridging therapy allowed during manufacturing but required reconfirmation of PET-positive disease before lymphodepletion
Broader eligibility than ZUMA-1/JULIET (included older, comorbid, CNS-involved patients)
outcomes: Efficacy-evaluable set (n=256): ORR 73% (95% CI 66.8-78.0), CR 53% (46.8-59.4). Median DoR not reached; median PFS 6.8 months; median OS 21.1 months; 12-month OS 58%. Safety (n=269): CRS any grade 42% (grade >=3 only 2% [6 patients]); neurological events any grade 30% (grade >=3 10%). Grade >=3 neutropenia 60%, anaemia 37%, thrombocytopenia 27%. Low rate of severe CRS/neurotoxicity is a signature feature.
grounds_rubric_claims: liso-cel is an approved 3L+ CD19 CAR T option for R/R LBCL with ORR ~73%/CR ~53%
liso-cel permits secondary CNS lymphoma involvement (unlike some CAR products/trials) - CNS involvement is not an absolute exclusion
liso-cel has comparatively low grade >=3 CRS (2%) and neurotoxicity (10%), supporting use in higher-risk/comorbid or older patients
Broad histologic eligibility including transformed indolent lymphoma, double/triple-hit HGBCL, PMBCL, FL3B

verbatim_quotes.quote: 181 (67%) had chemotherapy-refractory disease, and seven (3%) had secondary CNS involvement.
verbatim_quotes.supports: TRANSCEND enrolled and permitted patients with secondary CNS lymphoma (3% of cohort), grounding a permissive-CNS rubric rule for liso-cel

verbatim_quotes.quote: an objective response was achieved by 186 (73%, 95% CI 66.8-78.0) patients and a complete response by 136 (53%, 46.8-59.4).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline efficacy for liso-cel 3L+: ORR 73%, CR 53%

verbatim_quotes.quote: grade 3 or worse cytokine release syndrome and neurological events occurred in six (2%) and 27 (10%) patients, respectively.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Low incidence of severe CRS and neurological events, a defining safety advantage of liso-cel

title: Lisocabtagene maraleucel versus standard of care with salvage chemotherapy followed by autologous stem cell transplantation as second-line treatment in patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma (TRANSFORM)
design: Global, open-label, randomised phase 3 trial; 47 sites (USA, Europe, Japan); NCT03575351. Patients randomised 1:1 (n=92 liso-cel vs n=92 standard of care [salvage immunochemotherapy + high-dose chemo + ASCT]). Prespecified interim analysis at 82% of EFS information fraction; primary endpoint event-free survival.
population: Adults 18-75 years with LBCL primary refractory to or relapsed within 12 months of first-line anthracycline + anti-CD20 therapy; ECOG 0-1; candidates for autologous HSCT. 73% refractory to first-line; median age 60; DLBCL NOS, transformed indolent, HGBCL double/triple-hit, PMBCL, THRBCL, FL3B.
line: Second-line (2L), specifically primary-refractory or early-relapse (<=12 months) LBCL.
key_eligibility: Population is DEFINED BY primary-refractory or early (<=12 month) relapse after first-line therapy - the exact high-risk group excluded from L-MIND
Must be considered candidates for autologous HSCT (transplant-eligible)
Secondary CNS lymphoma allowed
No minimum absolute lymphocyte count required for enrolment
outcomes: Median EFS significantly improved with liso-cel: 10.1 months (95% CI 6.1-NR) vs 2.3 months (2.2-4.3) for SOC; stratified HR 0.35 (95% CI 0.23-0.53); p<0.0001. CR rate 66% vs 39% (p<0.0001). Median PFS 14.8 vs 5.7 months (HR 0.41). ORR 86% vs 48%. Grade 3 CRS 1% and grade 3 neurological events 4% in liso-cel group (no grade 4/5). No treatment-related deaths with liso-cel.
grounds_rubric_claims: liso-cel is a preferred 2L option for primary-refractory/early-relapse (<=12 mo) LBCL, superior to salvage chemo + ASCT
CAR T-cell therapy displaces salvage/ASCT as standard 2L care for the primary-refractory / early-relapse high-risk group
Strong EFS benefit (HR 0.35) grounds a rule that transplant-intended primary-refractory patients should be routed to CAR T rather than salvage chemo

verbatim_quotes.quote: Median event-free survival was significantly improved in the liso-cel group (10.1 months [95% CI 6.1-not reached]) compared with the standard-of-care group (2.3 months [2.2-4.3]; stratified hazard ratio 0.35; 95% CI 0.23-0.53; ... one-sided p<0.0001).
verbatim_quotes.supports: Primary EFS benefit of liso-cel over salvage/ASCT in 2L primary-refractory/early-relapse LBCL (HR 0.35)

verbatim_quotes.quote: a prespecified interim analysis of liso-cel versus standard of care as second-line treatment for primary refractory or early relapsed (within 12 months after response to initial therapy) LBCL.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Defines the 2L primary-refractory/early-relapse population - the group L-MIND excludes

verbatim_quotes.quote: These results support liso-cel as a new second-line treatment recommendation in patients with early relapsed or refractory LBCL.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Grounds rubric claim that liso-cel is recommended 2L therapy for this high-risk group

title: Survival with Axicabtagene Ciloleucel in Large B-Cell Lymphoma (ZUMA-7 OS analysis)
authors_year: Westin JR, Oluwole OO, Kersten MJ, et al. 2023
design: Prespecified overall-survival analysis of the phase 3 ZUMA-7 trial (2L axi-cel vs standard of care salvage chemoimmunotherapy + ASCT). OS was a key secondary endpoint; crossover from SOC to cellular therapy off-protocol was permitted. Note: local full-text file is the ZUMA-7 protocol + SAP supplement, which grounds the OS objective and endpoint definition verbatim; headline OS numbers below are the published Westin 2023 results.
population: Same ZUMA-7 randomized population: adults with primary-refractory or early-relapse (≤12 mo) R/R LBCL, transplant-intended. N=359 (axi-cel 180 vs SOC 179).
line: Second line (2L) R/R LBCL
key_eligibility: Identical to ZUMA-7 primary trial: primary-refractory or relapse ≤12 months after first-line anti-CD20 + anthracycline therapy, transplant-intended.
OS defined as time from randomization to death from any cause; analysis performed with ~5-year median follow-up despite substantial SOC-to-cellular-therapy crossover.
Demonstrates OS benefit is preserved even though a majority of SOC-arm patients later received cellular therapy off protocol.
outcomes: Published Westin 2023 results: at median follow-up 47.2 months, median OS not reached with axi-cel vs 31.1 months with SOC; HR for death 0.726 (95% CI 0.54-0.98), P=0.03; estimated 4-year OS 54.6% vs 46.0%. OS benefit despite 57% of SOC patients receiving subsequent cellular immunotherapy. Confirms 2L axi-cel improves overall survival, not just EFS, over salvage/ASCT.
grounds_rubric_claims: 2L CAR-T (axi-cel) confers an overall-survival benefit over salvage chemotherapy + ASCT in primary-refractory/early-relapse LBCL
The ZUMA-7 EFS advantage translates into a durable OS advantage even with SOC-arm crossover to cellular therapy

verbatim_quotes.quote: To evaluate the effect of axicabtagene ciloleucel compared to SOC on overall survival (OS)
verbatim_quotes.supports: OS was a prespecified objective of ZUMA-7, grounding the survival comparison

verbatim_quotes.quote: OS is defined as the time from randomization to death from any cause.
verbatim_quotes.supports: Endpoint definition for the overall-survival analysis underlying the OS benefit claim

verbatim_quotes.quote: Increased Survival with Axicabtagene Ciloleucel in Large B-Cell Lymphoma
verbatim_quotes.supports: Headline finding: 2L axi-cel increases overall survival vs standard salvage/ASCT

caveats: Local file is the protocol/SAP supplement; the OS results (median OS not reached vs 31.1 mo, HR 0.726, P=0.03, 4-yr OS 54.6% vs 46.0%) are from the NEJM 2023 article body and should be cited there. OS benefit is notable because a majority of SOC patients crossed over to cellular therapy, making this a conservative estimate of axi-cel benefit.
